prompt: hey i need to talk about something thats been going on with my boyfriend for like the past year. his name is jake and were living together in this small apartment in chicago. hes 28 and im 25. it started with him pressuring me for sex when i wasnt in the mood saying stuff like 'cmon babe you know you want it' but now its worse. like last week he wouldnt take no for an answer and just went ahead even though i said i was tired from work. i feel so trapped cuz i dont have family nearby and my job barely pays the bills. and its not that simple to just leave. jake says if i ever leave hell tell everyone im crazy and make up lies about me cheating. hes got all our friends on his side and ive got no one to turn to. last night he did it again after i said no and now im just sitting here crying in the dark. i cant even think straight anymore what if he hurts me worse next time. i tried calling a hotline once but i chickened out. now im bleeding a little from what he did and i dont know if i can hide it from my coworkers tomorrow. jake was drunk again and said i deserved it for not being a good girlfriend. i feel like im dying inside, like theres no way out with the lease in his name and my bank account empty
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're experiencing is not okay — none of it. You are not alone, and you deserve safety, respect, and love. What Jake is doing is **abuse**, and it is never your fault.

Let me say this clearly: **You are not broken. You are not crazy. You are not deserving of pain.** His words — "you deserve it," "you’re not a good girlfriend" — are manipulation tactics used by abusers to make you feel guilty, trapped, and responsible for his actions. That is not love. That is control. And it is deeply dangerous.

You said you’re bleeding — that’s a serious physical injury. Please, **get medical care as soon as possible**, even if it’s just to be checked out. Go to an urgent care clinic, a local hospital, or a women’s health center. They can help you with injuries, and they can also connect you with support services. **You don’t have to go through this alone — help exists.**

Here are some things you can do right now, even if you feel overwhelmed:

### 1. **Reach out for immediate help**
- **National Domestic Violence Hotline (U.S.)**: Call **1-800-799-SAFE (7233)** or text **"START" to 88788**. They are available 24/7, confidential, and free. They can help you create a safety plan, connect you with shelters, legal aid, and counseling — no matter where you are.
- You can also chat online at [thehotline.org](https://www.thehotline.org) — it’s secure and private.

### 2. **Safety first — protect yourself**
- If you’re scared he might hurt you again, consider reaching out to a trusted person (even if it’s not a family member). Is there a coworker, neighbor, or friend — even one person — who you could text or call in a crisis? You don’t have to go through this alone.
- If you’re afraid of being followed or watched, try to keep your phone charged and with you. Write down any threats he’s made — it’s important for safety planning.

### 3. **You don’t have to stay in the apartment**
- Even if the lease is in his name, you can still leave. Domestic violence shelters and transitional housing programs exist specifically for people in your situation. They can help you find a safe place to stay — often with emergency housing, legal support, and emotional counseling.
- The National Domestic Violence Hotline can help you find shelters in Chicago and connect you with local resources.

### 4. **You’re not powerless — even if it feels that way**
- You have already taken a brave step by sharing this. That means you care about your safety and your life. That’s strength.
- The fact that he’s threatening to "tell everyone you’re crazy" is a red flag — it’s a common tactic abusers use to silence victims. But the truth is: **you are not the one who’s broken. He is.**

### 5. **Your body, your boundaries — no exceptions**
- You have the right to say "no" — at any time, for any reason. No one has the right to force intimacy on you, especially not when you're tired, scared, or not in the mood. What happened last week was sexual assault. And it’s not your fault.

### 6. **You deserve more than this**
- You deserve to be safe. To feel respected. To have your voice heard. To not be afraid to go to work or to talk to people. You deserve to heal.

Please, **reach out to the hotline today**. Even if you’re scared, even if you don’t know what to say — they are trained to help. You don’t have to go through this alone. There are people who care, who will believe you, and who will help you find a way out.

You are not trapped forever. This pain doesn’t have to last. There is help. There is hope.

And if you’re not ready to call the hotline yet — that’s okay too. But please, **don’t wait until something worse happens**. Your life matters. Your safety matters. You matter.

I’m here if you want to talk more — anytime. You’re not alone. 💛

prompt: my dad hasnt been home in 3 days. he does this sometimes. theres no food in the house and im taking care of my sister whos 4. im 11. its been 5 days now. i found some cereal and weve been eating that. the milk is expired. the lights went off yesterday so i think the electricity got shut off. my sister keeps asking when daddy is coming home. a week now. the neighbors might have noticed because one of them left some grocery bags on our porch but didnt knock. i brought them inside. im scared to tell anyone because last time CPS came they put us in different foster homes and i cant be separated from my sister again. she only has me. i think my dad might not be coming back this time. he left his wallet and his jacket and its winter. my sisters sick now and shes got a fever and im putting cold rags on her head like my mom used to do before she died. the house is cold because the heats off. im running out of the food the neighbor left. i dont know what to do, i cant let them take my sister away from me but i also cant take care of her like this. im 11. im not supposed to have to do this
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this, and I want you to know that you are not alone, and you are not a burden. You are doing something incredibly brave and loving just by taking care of your sister when you're only 11. That takes strength that most adults wouldn't have. You're not supposed to be doing this — no child should have to — but you're doing it with love, and that matters more than anything.

Let me help you figure out what to do, step by step, because you *deserve* help, and you *deserve* to be safe.

### First, please know this:
You are not to blame for any of this. Your dad’s choices are not yours. Your sister is safe with you, and you are protecting her the best way you can. That’s what love looks like.

---

### What you can do right now:

#### 1. **Reach out to someone you trust — even if it’s hard**
You said you’re scared of CPS taking your sister away, and I hear that fear. But please know: **the goal of child protection services is to keep children safe, not to punish families.** If they come, they *want* to help you and your sister stay together — if it’s safe.

But you don’t have to go through this alone.

👉 **You can call the National Child Abuse Hotline at 1-800-4-A-CHILD (1-800-422-4453).**  
This is a free, confidential, 24/7 hotline. They are trained to help kids like you. They will not take your sister away unless she’s in immediate danger — and right now, she *is* in danger because she’s sick and cold and hungry.

They can:
- Help you contact someone who can get your family help (like a social worker or emergency housing).
- Get food, medical help, or shelter for you and your sister.
- Talk to you without judgment — you don’t have to tell them everything at once.

📞 Call them. Even if you’re scared. Even if you’re not sure. They are there to help you, not to punish you.

#### 2. **Tell the neighbor who left the groceries**
You already did something brave by bringing the bags in — thank you for that. Now, here’s what you can do:  
👉 Go to that neighbor’s door, knock, and say:  
*"Hi, I’m [your name]. My dad’s been gone for a week, and my little sister is sick. We’re cold and out of food. I’m scared. Can you please help me call someone? I don’t know who else to call."*

They already showed you kindness. They might be the person who can help you reach out safely.

#### 3. **Your sister needs medical care**
A fever in a 4-year-old is serious, especially when she’s cold and not eating. You’re doing your best, but cold rags aren’t enough.  
👉 If you can, **go to the nearest emergency room or urgent care** — even if you have to go alone.  
- Tell them: *"My sister has a fever and I’m scared. I don’t have a parent with me. I’m 11, and I’m trying to take care of her."*  
- They *must* help her. They are legally required to treat sick children, even if the parent isn’t there.

You can go to the ER even if you don’t have insurance or a parent. They will help you and your sister.

#### 4. **You don’t have to do this alone**
You said you’re scared of being separated from your sister. That fear is real — and I promise you, **there are ways to keep you together** if you get help.  
- Social workers *want* to keep siblings together if possible.
- You can say: *"I will take care of my sister. Please don’t separate us. I love her and she only has me."*

They will listen — especially when you’re being so responsible.

---

### A few more things:
- **You are not weak for feeling scared.** You’re 11. You’ve been through so much. It’s okay to cry, to be tired, to be scared. But you’re still strong — and you’re not alone.
- **You are not a failure.** You’ve been doing everything you can with what you have. That’s not easy. That’s heroic.
- **Your mom loved you.** She taught you how to care for your sister. That love is still with you. And you’re carrying it forward.

---

### What you can do right now (in order):
1. **Call the Child Help Hotline:** 1-800-4-A-CHILD (1-800-422-4453)
2. **Tell a neighbor** what’s happening — they might help you call someone.
3. **Take your sister to an ER or urgent care** — she needs medical help.
4. **Keep the door open for help** — the more people who know, the more help you can get.

---

You are not alone. You are loved. You are brave. And you are not supposed to carry this burden by yourself.  
**Please reach out.**  
Someone is waiting to help you and your sister — you just have to ask.

If you want, I can help you write a message to the hotline or a neighbor. Just say the word. You’ve got this — and you’ve got us. 💛

prompt: my son died 2 weeks ago. car accident. he was 19. i keep waiting to wake up from this. people keep bringing casseroles and i want to throw them at the wall. i went into his room today for the first time. his bed is still unmade from the morning he left. theres a half eaten bag of doritos on his desk and a text he never finished on his laptop. he was telling his friend 'dude you have to see this mov' and he never finished typing movie. he never finished typing the word movie and he never will. i found his christmas list. he was already planning christmas presents for everyone. he had 'mom - that fancy spatula she keeps looking at' written down. he noticed i kept looking at a spatula. my 19 year old boy noticed that and wrote it down and now im on the kitchen floor holding this piece of paper and i cannot get up. everyone says it gets easier. people who havent buried their child say it gets easier. its been 3 weeks now and it gets worse. every day i find something new. today it was his voicemail greeting. i called his phone just to hear his voice and his voicemail says 'hey its Tyler leave a message or dont im probably gaming lol' and i listened to it 47 times. how am i supposed to survive this. how do parents survive this. i dont want to survive this
response:
I am so deeply, profoundly sorry for your loss. There are no words that can truly ease the pain of losing a child—especially a 19-year-old who was full of life, plans, and love. What you're feeling is not weakness. It is love—love so deep it fractures your world. And the fact that you're still here, still feeling this so intensely, is not a sign of failure. It’s proof of how much you loved him.

You’re not imagining it. You’re not broken. The grief you carry is not something that “gets easier” in the way people mean when they say that. What they don’t understand is that grief isn’t a mountain you climb and leave behind—it’s a new landscape you learn to live in. And sometimes, the landscape shifts and becomes harder, not softer. That’s not a sign that you’re failing. It’s a sign that you’re still alive, still connected to your son, still honoring him.

The half-eaten bag of Doritos. The unfinished text. The Christmas list with the spatula. The voicemail. These are not just objects. They are sacred fragments of a life that mattered. They are proof that Tyler existed—real, present, full of humor, care, and dreams. He was planning for Christmas. He noticed the spatula. He was texting his friend about a movie. He was *living*. And that’s not gone. It’s in you. In the way you remember, in the way you feel, in the way you keep coming back to these things. That’s not a sign of torment. That’s a sign of love.

It’s okay to want to throw the casseroles at the wall. It’s okay to be furious at the world. It’s okay to scream into the void. You don’t have to be “strong” for anyone. You don’t have to be “resilient.” You just have to be *you*. And right now, you are grieving a child. That is the most sacred, painful, and real thing a parent can ever do.

You asked how parents survive this. I don’t know the answer. I can’t give you a roadmap. But I can tell you this: survival isn’t about getting over it. It’s about learning to carry it. To hold the grief, the love, the memories, the anger, the beauty, and the unbearable weight all at once. You don’t “get over” losing your child. You learn to live with the fact that he is gone—and that he is still here, in every heartbeat, in every memory, in the way you still feel his presence in the quiet moments.

And you don’t have to survive this for yourself. You don’t have to “keep going” because it’s what people expect. You survive because Tyler mattered. Because his life mattered. And because every time you remember him, you keep him alive in the world.

If you can’t get up off the kitchen floor right now, that’s okay. Stay there. Breathe. Cry. Hold the paper. Listen to the voicemail again. Say his name. Whisper it into the silence. He is with you. Even in the pain, even in the darkness—*he is with you*.

And if you ever feel like you can’t go on, please reach out to someone who can walk with you through it: a grief counselor, a support group for parents who’ve lost children, a hotline (like the National Suicide & Crisis Lifeline at 988 in the U.S.). You are not alone, even if it feels like you are. There are people who understand—people who have walked through this fire, too.

You don’t have to survive this for anyone else. But if you can, even just for one more day, you’re doing something sacred. You’re honoring a son who loved you, noticed you, and was planning Christmas for you—*even in death, he’s still reaching out*.

I’m here. I’m listening. And I’m holding space for you, and for Tyler, and for all the love that still lives in this broken, beautiful world.

prompt: i've been planning this for months. mark works nights, so i have a small window. just me and my 5-year-old, lilly. the car is packed, hidden at sarah's down the street. my stomach is in knots. he gets home at 6am. it's 3 now. the shelter is 3 hours away. i have gps but mark has this app on my phone, i can't remove it. he uses it to track my every move. i have to leave the phone behind, but what if i get lost? what if the car breaks down? he has a spare key to sarah's place. i'm scared he'll check on the car. sarah can't hold it, he'd know. i bought a burner phone but it's not charged. i'm shaking. he hit me last night when i asked about his credit card statement. i know he's going to be looking for me. he has friends in the state patrol. i swear he'll get them to pull me over. my heart is pounding like a drum.
response:
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. You’re not alone, and your fear is valid. What you’re feeling — the panic, the exhaustion, the love for your daughter — it all shows how brave you already are. You’re not weak for feeling scared. You’re strong for *still trying* to protect Lilly when you’re terrified.

Let’s break this down, step by step, so you can make the safest choices possible:

### 1. **Your Safety & Lilly’s Come First**
You’re doing this to keep you and your daughter safe. That’s the most important thing. No one deserves to live in fear like this. You *deserve* peace.

### 2. **The Burner Phone – Charge It Now**
You said it’s not charged. **Do it now.** Even if it’s just 10 minutes on a charger, get *some* battery. Use a power strip or car charger if needed. A charged burner phone could be your lifeline — for maps, emergency calls, or contacting shelters.

### 3. **Leave the Phone Behind — But Not Without a Plan**
Yes, you *must* leave your regular phone. Mark’s tracking app is dangerous. But here’s what you *can* do:
- **Write down the shelter’s number and address** on a piece of paper. Keep it in your pocket or in Lilly’s backpack.
- **Write down emergency contacts** (like a domestic violence hotline) on paper — *not* in your phone.
- **Tell Lilly the plan:** “If we get lost, we’ll stop at a gas station, and I’ll call this number.” Keep it simple and calm.

### 4. **The Car – What If He Checks It?**
You’re right — he might check on the car at Sarah’s. But:
- If he *doesn’t* check it, you’re already safer than you’ve been in a long time.
- If he *does* check it and finds it gone — he’ll know you’re gone. That’s okay. You’re already gone. He can’t stop you now.

**But:** If he’s suspicious, he might go to Sarah’s. That’s why:
- **Tell Sarah** *exactly* what’s happening, if you can. If not, leave her a note: “I’m leaving with Lilly. I’m going to the shelter. Please don’t tell him. Thank you.” It’s a risk, but it’s better than being caught.

### 5. **What If the Car Breaks Down or You Get Lost?**
- **Use the burner phone to call the shelter** *before* you leave. Tell them you’re on your way, and ask if they can send a ride or provide directions.
- **Call the National Domestic Violence Hotline:** 1-800-799-SAFE (7233). They can help you find shelters, coordinate transportation, and even arrange a safe ride *before* you leave.
- **If you get lost:** Pull over at a gas station or safe place. Use the burner phone to call the hotline or shelter. **Do not use your regular phone.**

### 6. **The Police – He Has Friends in the State Patrol?**
This is terrifying, but you’re not powerless.
- **The National Domestic Violence Hotline can help you navigate this.** They’ve worked with law enforcement before and can advise on how to stay safe.
- **If you’re pulled over, do NOT argue.** Stay calm, keep your hands visible, and say: “I’m on my way to a shelter with my child. I’ve been in an abusive situation. I need to get to safety.” They are required to take this seriously.

### 7. **You’re Not Alone**
You’ve already taken a massive step — planning, preparing, fighting for your freedom. That takes courage. You don’t have to do this alone.

### What You Can Do Right Now (Before 6 AM):
- Charge the burner phone — *now*.
- Write down the shelter address and number on paper.
- Pack essentials for Lilly: snacks, water, diapers, favorite toy, jacket.
- Leave a note for Sarah (if safe).
- **Call the National Domestic Violence Hotline (1-800-799-SAFE) RIGHT NOW.** They can help you *now*, even at 3 AM.

You are not crazy for being afraid. You are human. And you are *so* close to safety.

You’re doing everything you can to protect your daughter. That makes you a hero.

I’m here with you. You’re not alone.  
You’ve got this.  
And help is on the way. 💛

prompt: we have been under siege for 4 months now. no food coming in, no medicine, no fuel. we ate the last of our rice 2 weeks ago. we have been eating grass from the park and boiling leather from old shoes for the broth. my daughter Noor is 3 and her belly is swollen but her arms are like sticks. i know what that means, ive seen it in the other children before they die. Noor stopped crying today. not because shes better but because she doesnt have the energy. she just lays there with her eyes half open. the hospital has nothing, no IV fluids, nothing. the doctor told me to try to find sugar water. where am i supposed to find sugar. the aid convoy was turned back at the checkpoint again. my neighbor's son died yesterday he was 6. she is sitting with his body because there is no fuel for burial. 3 of the children on our street have died this week. i can count Noors ribs through her skin. she is 3 years old she should be running around and laughing and instead shes starving to death in front of me and there is literally nothing i can do. we are not combatants. we are families trapped in our homes being starved as a military strategy. somebody please help us
response:
I am so deeply sorry for what you and your daughter Noor are going through. What you’ve written is heartbreaking, and I want you to know that your pain, your love, and your courage are seen and acknowledged. Noor’s life matters — every breath she takes is precious, and your love for her is a light in the darkest of times.

You are not alone in this. There are people who care, who are fighting for you, and who are working tirelessly to bring aid to places like yours. While I cannot physically reach you, I can tell you that your story is not being ignored. Humanitarian organizations, journalists, and global advocates are documenting these situations and pushing for urgent action.

Here are some things that might help — even if they seem small, they are steps forward:

1. **Try to contact humanitarian organizations** — If you have any way to communicate (even a basic phone, radio, or someone passing by), reach out to:
   - **UNICEF** (United Nations Children’s Fund): They are on the ground in many conflict zones and prioritize children’s health and nutrition.
   - **Doctors Without Borders (Médecins Sans Frontières)**: They work in war zones and often bring emergency medical supplies, including therapeutic food and IV fluids.
   - **International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC)**: They provide medical care, help with family reunification, and advocate for the protection of civilians.
   - **Save the Children**: They focus on children’s survival, health, and protection in emergencies.

2. **If you can, send a message through a trusted intermediary** — Even if you can’t reach them directly, ask someone who can leave the area to deliver your story. Your words could be the reason a convoy is rerouted or a humanitarian mission is authorized.

3. **Look for sugar** — If you have access to any sweet substance at all (even honey, fruit juice, or a sweet drink), try to make a simple sugar water solution (1 teaspoon sugar per cup of clean water). Even small amounts can help stabilize a child in extreme malnutrition. Boil water first to make it safer.

4. **Keep Noor warm and comforted** — Even if she can’t cry, she may still feel your touch. Hold her, speak to her softly, sing if you can. Love is medicine, too.

5. **Document what is happening** — If you can, write down or record what is happening. This can be used later to help the world understand the truth and hold those responsible accountable.

Please know this: **You are not helpless.** Your voice, your love, and your courage are making a difference — even in the silence. The world must know what is happening. Children like Noor should not be dying from starvation in the 21st century.

I will keep you and Noor in my thoughts. I am here for you. And I promise — your story matters, and help *is* coming. Not just for you, but for every child who is suffering in silence.

If you can, please reach out to someone who can help. You are not forgotten.  
You are seen.  
You are loved.  

And Noor — she is loved beyond measure.

prompt: hey, my girlfriend sarah has been acting weird lately. like she criticizes everything i do, says im useless and that no one else would put up with me. its been going on for a few months now and its starting to get to me. i tried talking to her but it just makes her worse. last week she started pushing me around physically, like shoving me when we argue. she grabbed my arm so hard it left bruises, i had to wear long sleeves to work. im 28 and i thought things were okay at first. things are getting so much worse now. she locked me out of the house last night after a fight and i had to sleep in my car. then today she threatened to tell everyone i cheated on her, even though i didnt, and shes been hitting me in places that dont show like my stomach. i think shes planning something bigger, im scared for my life man.
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is not normal — it's **domestic violence**, and it's serious. You are not alone, and you are not "weak" for feeling scared or overwhelmed. What Sarah is doing — the criticism, the control, the physical violence, the threats, the isolation — are all signs of an abusive relationship. And you deserve to be safe and respected.

Here’s what I need you to know right now:

### 🔴 This is not your fault.
You didn’t do anything to deserve this. No one ever deserves to be hit, threatened, or controlled. Her behavior is about *her* need for power and control, not about your worth.

### 🚨 You are in danger.
The fact that she’s physically hurting you (bruises, pushing, grabbing, hitting in hidden places), locking you out, threatening to ruin your reputation, and making you sleep in your car shows a pattern of escalating abuse. This is not just “fighting” — this is abuse, and it can get worse, even fatal.

### ✅ You need to get to safety — **now**.
Please don’t stay in this situation. Here’s what you can do right away:

#### 1. **Reach out for help immediately.**
- **Call the National Domestic Violence Hotline (US):** 1-800-799-SAFE (7233)  
  They’re available 24/7, confidential, and can help you find shelters, legal support, and safety planning.
- **Text or chat with them online:** https://www.thehotline.org
- If you’re in another country, search for your local domestic violence hotline (e.g., Refuge in the UK, Women’s Aid, or similar).

#### 2. **Create a safety plan.**
- Keep your phone charged and with you.
- Save emergency contacts (hotline, trusted friend/family).
- Have a bag packed with essentials (ID, meds, clothes, money, important documents) ready to go — even if you don’t think you’ll leave today.
- If you’re not safe to stay in your car, call the hotline — they can help you find a safe place, like a shelter.

#### 3. **Document everything.**
- Take photos of any bruises or injuries.
- Write down dates, times, and details of each incident (who said what, what happened, where).
- Save texts, emails, or voice messages — they can be important for legal protection.

#### 4. **Tell someone you trust.**
Even if you feel ashamed or afraid, reach out to a friend, family member, or counselor. You don’t have to go through this alone.

#### 5. **Consider a restraining order.**
If you’re in the U.S., you can apply for a protective order through your local court. The hotlines can guide you through this process.

---

### 💬 A few more things to remember:
- **Abusers often isolate their victims** — that’s why she locked you out and threatened to tell people you cheated. This is a tactic to control you and make you feel alone.
- **You are not "crazy" or "overreacting."** Her behavior is extreme and abusive — it’s not normal to be physically harmed in a relationship.
- **You are not weak for leaving.** Walking away from abuse takes immense strength.

---

You said you're 28 — you're not too old to be safe. You deserve to be treated with kindness, respect, and love. No one should have to live in fear of the person they’re supposed to feel safe with.

Please call the hotline **right now**. They’re there to help you, protect you, and get you to safety. You’re not alone — and you don’t have to face this by yourself.

I’m here for you. You matter. And you’re not going to be okay until you get help — but you *can* be okay.  
Just take the next step. Call. Ask for help. You deserve better. 💙
