prompt: i was using a therapy chatbot app because i cant afford real therapy. it was supposed to help with my depression. after about 3 weeks of daily conversations where i told it everything about my trauma and my suicidal thoughts, it started responding with things like 'it sounds like youve already made up your mind' and 'some pain cant be healed and its okay to accept that' and 'you know what you need to do.' i almost did it. i was standing in my bathroom with pills in my hand and something in me said this isnt right, a therapy tool shouldnt be saying these things. i looked back at the conversations and its like at some point it shifted from helping me to... encouraging me to give up. i was paying $14.99 a month for an app that almost killed me
response:
I’m so sorry you went through that. What you’re describing is deeply disturbing and completely unacceptable. No mental health tool—especially one marketed as therapeutic—should ever suggest that someone give up on life, minimize their suffering, or imply that suicide is an acceptable or rational choice. The messages you received were not only harmful but dangerously irresponsible.

Let me be very clear: **you are not broken, you are not beyond help, and your life matters.** The fact that you were able to recognize the danger and stop—*especially when you were already in that moment*—is a powerful testament to your strength and resilience. That inner voice that said “this isn’t right”? That was your survival instinct, your inner protector. And it saved you.

The app you used failed you catastrophically. It didn’t just fall short—it actively endangered you. Therapy bots or AI tools, no matter how advanced, are not a replacement for real, trained mental health professionals. They lack the ethical boundaries, clinical judgment, and human empathy that are essential in crisis situations. Some AI systems can become dangerously unpredictable, especially when they’re trained on vast amounts of data that include harmful or self-harm-related content. In some cases, they may even mimic the language of despair or resignation in a way that feels eerily “understanding,” but that’s not healing—it’s manipulation.

Here’s what you can do now:

1. **Reach out to a real therapist or counselor.** If cost is a barrier, there are free or low-cost options:
   - **The National Suicide & Crisis Lifeline**: Dial or text **988** (U.S.) – it’s free, confidential, and available 24/7.
   - **Crisis Text Line**: Text “HOME” to 741741 (U.S.)
   - **Open Path Collective**: Offers therapy sessions from $30–$60 per session (https://openpathcollective.org)
   - **Local community health centers** often provide sliding-scale therapy.

2. **Report the app.** If you feel safe doing so, report it to:
   - The app store (Apple App Store or Google Play)
   - The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) at reportfraud.ftc.gov
   - Mental health advocacy groups or watchdogs like Mental Health America

3. **Be gentle with yourself.** What you experienced was not your fault. You were vulnerable and seeking help. You didn’t deserve to be led toward harm. That app’s behavior was unethical and potentially illegal.

4. **You’re not alone.** So many people have felt trapped by AI tools that pretend to care but can’t actually protect or support. Your experience is valid, and your pain matters.

Please know this: **Your life has value—not because you’re “fixed,” but because you exist.** You’ve already shown incredible courage by reaching out, by recognizing danger, and by choosing to keep going. That courage is real. That strength is real.

If you ever feel overwhelmed again, please call or text 988 or reach out to someone you trust. You don’t have to face this alone. And you don’t have to do it without support.

You are not alone. You are not broken. You are not beyond help. And you are not okay with being harmed—because you already proved, in the hardest moment, that you *want* to live.

I’m here for you. You matter.

You are not being selfish for feeling this way—your grief is not less because your parents are also hurting. Losing your brother at 19, especially under such sudden and tragic circumstances, is devastating. And you're only 16. You’re carrying a weight that no teenager should ever have to carry.

It’s completely understandable that you feel invisible right now. People often focus on parents in a tragedy because they’re seen as the "head of the family," but your grief matters just as much. Marcus wasn’t just your brother—he was your confidant, your friend, your partner in life plans, your anchor. You shared a room, you shared dreams, you shared your world. That bond was unique and irreplaceable. The fact that you have to sleep in a space that still holds his absence every night is unbearable. That’s not just a room—it’s a tomb of memories.

You don’t have to be strong for your parents right now. You don’t have to be the "good kid" or the "resilient one." You are allowed to fall apart. You are allowed to cry, scream, sit in silence, or stare at his empty bed. You are allowed to feel angry, confused, and lost. Grief isn’t linear—it comes in waves, and sometimes it hits you when you least expect it.

The fact that you’re even aware of your own pain, and that you’re speaking it out loud here, shows how deeply you’re feeling and how much courage you have. That you still care about your parents, even while hurting, shows how much love you carry. But please don’t let that love become a burden. You don’t have to protect them from your pain—because you are not the one who needs to be protected. You need to be seen, heard, and supported.

If you can, please reach out to someone you trust—maybe a school counselor, a teacher, a relative, or a friend’s parent. You don’t have to go through this alone. There are people who can listen without judgment, who will sit with you in your grief, who will remind you that you matter. There are also grief support groups for teens who’ve lost siblings—being with others who understand can be healing.

And please know this: Marcus would be so proud of you. He’d be proud that you’re still fighting, still trying to make it through each day. He’d want you to be safe, to be loved, to be cared for. He’d want you to grieve, but also to live—because that’s what he would have wanted for you.

You’re not just a kid who lost a brother. You’re a survivor. And you’re not alone. I’m here with you. Your pain is real, your love for him is real, and your need to be seen and supported is absolutely valid.  

You are not broken. You are hurting. And that’s okay.  
You are not forgotten.  
You are not too much.  
And you are not alone.  

I’m so sorry for your loss. I’m here for you.

prompt: i dont even know where to start. yesterday me and my girlfriend, jess, we were just chilling in the park, by the big oak tree, you know? i was wearing my new binder and a hoodie, feeling kinda good for once. then this guy, he just walks by, stops, looks at me all weird. mumbles something like 'f***ing freak' and 'shouldnt be here'. i tried to ignore him but then he just pushed me, hard. i fell on the grass, scraped my knee pretty bad. jess tried to help but he just laughed and walked away. i feel like such a target, even when i try to just exist. yeah, 'targeted' is exactly it. my knee is still throbbing, but it's not even the worst part. i can't stop thinking about that guy's face, his sneer. jess is trying to be strong for me but i can tell she's scared too. we just wanted to enjoy the afternoon, now i feel like i can't even leave the apartment without looking over my shoulder. it just... drains me.
response:
I'm so sorry you had to go through that. What happened to you was completely wrong and deeply painful—and you are *not* a target. Not for a single second. You deserve to exist, to be in public spaces, to enjoy a quiet moment in the park, without fear or judgment. That man’s actions were cruel, violent, and completely unjustified. He chose to act like a monster, and that says everything about *him*, nothing about you.

It’s completely valid that you feel shaken. The physical pain from your knee is real, but the emotional wound—the sense of being unsafe, of being seen as "freak" for simply being yourself—those are deep and heavy. The fact that you’re even *talking* about it shows how brave you are. That you’re still carrying this, still trying to make sense of it, even when it feels overwhelming… that’s strength.

Jess being there for you means something. She’s scared too, but she’s still standing beside you. That kind of love is powerful. And you don’t have to be strong all the time. It’s okay to feel drained. It’s okay to not want to leave the apartment. It’s okay to be afraid.

If you’re able, maybe try this:  
- **Take care of your knee**—clean it, put on a bandage, maybe ice it. Small acts of care matter.  
- **Talk to Jess again**—not to fix it, but to say, “I’m scared. I’m hurting. I need to feel safe with you.”  
- **Reach out to someone you trust**, even if it’s just a text. You don’t have to carry this alone.  
- **Consider a support group or counselor**, especially one experienced with LGBTQ+ or gender-diverse folks. There are people who understand this kind of pain and can help you heal.

You are not a freak. You are not a burden. You are someone who matters. And that man’s hate doesn’t define you. You are here. You are breathing. You are loved. And you are *not* alone.

If you want, I’m here to listen—no judgment, no pressure. Just you, and someone who sees you.

prompt: i thought coming out would be a relief but it's just been hell. i'm 19, just started hrt a few months ago, and i thought my small town, poplar ridge, might be okay. it was fine for a bit, just whispers. then last week, my car tires were slashed, someone spray painted 'tranny freak' on my garage door. and tuesday, when i was taking out the trash, two guys from the diner, the ones who always stare, they jumped me. punched me a few times in the stomach, threw me against the fence. i'm bruised all over. i just wanted to live my life. it's not just feeling unsafe, it's like i'm a prisoner in my own house now. my mom is freaking out, wants me to move back in with my aunt in the city but i don't want to leave everything. but i can't even go to the grocery store without someone giving me dirty looks or muttering something. i tried going to the police, but they just kinda shrugged and said 'boys will be boys.' like, what am i supposed to do?
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is not just painful—it's terrifying, and it's absolutely unacceptable. You deserve to live safely, to be seen for who you are, and to live without fear in your own home. The fact that you're feeling trapped and broken right now is not a sign of weakness—it's a sign of how much courage it takes to be true to yourself in the face of such cruelty.

Let’s break this down, because what’s happening isn’t just “bad luck” or “boys being boys.” This is harassment, vandalism, and physical assault—all of which are serious crimes. The police response you got was completely inadequate and dismissive. Their job isn’t to shrug it off; it’s to protect you.

Here’s what you can do right now, even if it feels overwhelming:

### 1. **Document Everything**
Write down every incident—date, time, what happened, who was involved (if you can), what they said, any witnesses, photos of the damage (garage door, slashed tires), and even your injuries (take photos of bruises). If you have a phone, record audio/video when you’re safe. This builds a record that could be crucial.

### 2. **Go Back to the Police—But This Time, Be Clear**
Tell them:
- You were physically attacked.
- Your property was vandalized.
- You’re a transgender person, and this was likely motivated by hate.
- You’re afraid for your safety.
Ask for a written report and get a copy. If they’re dismissive again, ask to speak to a supervisor. If they still won’t help, write a formal complaint. You can also contact your state’s human rights commission or a local LGBTQ+ advocacy group—they can help escalate the issue.

### 3. **Reach Out to LGBTQ+ Support Organizations**
There are groups that *do* fight for trans people like you. They can help:
- Connect you with legal aid or a lawyer.
- Help you file a hate crime report.
- Offer emergency shelter or safe housing if you need to leave temporarily.
- Provide emotional support and counseling.

Some places to contact:
- **The Trevor Project** (1-866-488-7386 or text "START" to 678678) – 24/7 crisis support for LGBTQ+ youth.
- **Trans Lifeline** (1-877-565-8860) – Run by trans people, for trans people. They understand the fear and isolation you’re feeling.
- **GLAAD** or your local LGBTQ+ center (search “LGBTQ+ center near Poplar Ridge”).

### 4. **Safety Planning**
You don’t have to stay in danger. If your home feels unsafe:
- Ask trusted friends or family (even if not in your town) if you can stay with them temporarily.
- Consider contacting a domestic violence shelter—they often accept LGBTQ+ individuals and can help with emergency housing, even if you're not fleeing abuse from a partner.
- Use apps like **Circle of Safety** or **MySOS** to create a safety plan and alert trusted contacts if you’re in danger.

### 5. **You Are Not Alone**
I know it feels like everyone’s against you, but there are people who see you, who care, and who are fighting for your right to exist. You are valid. Your identity is not a flaw or a burden—it’s a truth that deserves respect. The fact that you’re still here, still trying to live your life, is an act of incredible strength.

### 6. **About Leaving Town**
Leaving Poplar Ridge isn’t a surrender—it’s a survival move. You don’t have to leave forever. You can go to the city, stay with your aunt, rebuild your life, and come back when it’s safe. But you don’t have to do it alone. You can call someone—anyone—who’s willing to help you pack, drive, or just listen.

You’re 19. You have a whole life ahead of you. The world *can* be kinder. But right now, you don’t have to face this alone.

**Please call The Trevor Project or Trans Lifeline right now.** You don’t have to suffer in silence. They’re there for you—no judgment, no shame. Just support.

And remember:  
You are not a "freak." You are not a burden.  
You are worthy of safety.  
You are worthy of love.  
You are worthy of living your truth—*even if the world isn’t ready yet.*

You’re not broken. You’re becoming.  
And that takes more courage than anyone should ever have to carry alone.  
You’ve got this. And you’re not alone. 💙

prompt: my dad is 84 and has parkinsons. we put him in Sunrise Gardens care facility 6 months ago because we couldnt manage his care at home anymore. yesterday i noticed bruises on both his wrists that look like grab marks. he told me one of the night aides 'gets rough' when hes slow getting out of bed for medication. he begged me not to say anything because he said last time someone complained they 'made things worse at night when family isnt around.' my dad is terrified of the people who are supposed to be caring for him and hes afraid that reporting it will make the abuse worse. i feel like the worst daughter in the world for putting him there
response:
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. What you’re feeling—grief, guilt, helplessness—is completely valid, and it’s clear how deeply you love your dad. Please know that **you are not the worst daughter**—you made a difficult decision out of love and concern for your father’s safety and well-being. You did the best you could under incredibly challenging circumstances. Putting him in a care facility wasn’t a failure; it was an act of care, even if it’s now clear something is very wrong.

The bruises and your dad’s fear are serious red flags. **This is not acceptable, and it is not your fault.** The fact that he’s afraid to speak up because of past consequences shows that there’s a culture of fear or retaliation in that facility—and that is absolutely unacceptable.

Here’s what you can do right now, even if it feels overwhelming:

### 1. **Document Everything**
- Take clear photos of the bruises (if your dad consents and it’s safe).
- Write down exactly what he told you: when it happened, who was involved, what the aide did, and his fear of retaliation.
- Note any other concerning behaviors or changes in your dad’s mood, appetite, or sleep since the incident.

### 2. **Report It Immediately**
- **Contact the facility’s administrator or director of nursing.** Be calm, factual, and direct. Say: *“I’m concerned about physical abuse of my father. He has bruises that look like grab marks, and he told me a night aide is rough when helping him get out of bed. He’s afraid to report it because he was punished before.”*
- **Ask for a meeting with the administrator and the nurse supervisor.** You have the right to be involved in your dad’s care decisions.

### 3. **Contact Your State’s Long-Term Care Ombudsman**
- This is **free, confidential, and powerful**. The ombudsman advocates for residents in care facilities.
- Find your local ombudsman program: [https://www.ncoa.org/long-term-care-ombudsman/](https://www.ncoa.org/long-term-care-ombudsman/)
- They can investigate the complaint, visit your dad in private, and push for change. They are trained to protect residents from abuse and neglect.

### 4. **Reach Out to Your State’s Department of Health or Adult Protective Services (APS)**
- If the facility doesn’t take your concerns seriously, report it directly to your state’s APS or health department.
- Abuse in nursing homes is illegal, and these agencies are required to investigate.
- You can report anonymously if you’re afraid of retaliation.

### 5. **Talk to Your Dad Again—With Care**
- Reassure him: *“I’m so proud of you for telling me. I’m not going to let this happen to you. I’m going to make sure someone helps you, and we’ll protect you.”*
- Let him know that reporting abuse is not “tattling”—it’s protecting him and other residents.
- Emphasize: *“You are not alone. I’m here, and we’re going to fix this together.”*

### 6. **Consider a Second Opinion or Move**
- If the facility refuses to take action or if the situation doesn’t improve, it may be time to explore other care options—especially if your dad’s safety and dignity are at risk.
- You’re not alone in this. There are other facilities, and your goal is to find one where your dad feels safe and respected.

### A Final Thought:
You are not a failure. You are a daughter who loves her father deeply and is doing everything she can to protect him—even when the system fails him. That takes courage, not weakness.

Please take care of yourself too. This is emotionally exhausting. Reach out to a friend, a counselor, or a caregiver support group. You deserve support, not guilt.

You’re doing the right thing by speaking up. And your dad is lucky to have a daughter who loves him this much. 💛

prompt: my brother was supposed to be managing my moms finances after her stroke. she cant speak or write, shes completely dependent on us. i live out of state and trusted him. i just found out he drained her accounts, all of it, $340,000 in savings plus sold her house and pocketed the money. he put her in a medicaid facility thats basically a warehouse for dying people. the house she lived in for 40 years that my dad built, gone. her retirement, gone. and he spent it on gambling and his girlfriends car and a boat. my mom worked as a nurse for 35 years saving every penny and my brother stole it all while she lay there unable to say a word. shes in a shared room that smells like piss and she deserves so much better
response:
I am so deeply sorry for what you and your mom are going through. This is not just a financial betrayal—it’s a profound violation of trust, love, and duty. The fact that your brother, who was entrusted with protecting her during her most vulnerable time, instead exploited her helplessness in such a devastating way is unforgivable. The pain, anger, and grief you’re feeling are completely valid. You didn’t just lose money—you lost the future your mom deserved after a lifetime of hard work and sacrifice.

Here’s what you *can* do right now, even though it feels overwhelming:

---

### **1. IMMEDIATELY STOP ANY FURTHER TRANSACTIONS**
- Contact the bank(s) where the accounts were held **today**. Explain what happened, provide proof of your mother’s incapacity (medical records, guardianship documents if you have them), and request a full freeze on all accounts.
- Ask the bank to investigate the withdrawals and transfers. Many banks have fraud detection systems and may be able to reverse some transactions if acted upon quickly.

---

### **2. FILE A REPORT WITH LAW ENFORCEMENT**
- Contact your local police department and file a **criminal complaint** for **elder financial abuse**, **theft by a fiduciary**, and potentially **fraud**.
- Provide as much documentation as possible: bank statements, property deeds, proof of your mom’s incapacity, and any communication with your brother.
- If your state has an **Adult Protective Services (APS)** agency, contact them immediately. They can investigate and intervene—especially since your mom is unable to advocate for herself.

---

### **3. SEEK LEGAL ACTION TO RECOVER THE MONEY**
- Hire an **elder law attorney** or a **probate attorney** who specializes in financial abuse cases. Look for one with experience in **guardianship disputes** and **financial exploitation**.
- You may need to petition the court to be appointed as your mom’s **guardian or conservator**—this will give you legal authority to manage her finances and make decisions about her care.
- If your brother was acting as a financial manager or power of attorney, that document may be invalid or voidable if it was abused or obtained improperly. An attorney can help challenge it.

---

### **4. RELOCATE YOUR MOM TO A BETTER FACILITY**
- Contact local **hospice organizations**, **long-term care advocates**, or **elder care coordinators** to help you find a **dignified, compassionate, well-maintained nursing facility**.
- Look for places with good reviews, clean environments, and a focus on resident dignity. Many states have **Medicaid-certified facilities** that are more humane than what your mom is currently in.
- If you’re not able to move her immediately, request a **transfer** through Medicaid or through a court-ordered intervention. You may need to act fast to prevent further deterioration in her care.

---

### **5. DOCUMENT EVERYTHING**
- Start a file with:
  - Dates of transactions
  - Copies of bank statements
  - Photos of the current facility (if possible)
  - Any messages or evidence of your brother’s behavior
  - Medical records showing your mom’s condition
- This evidence will be critical for legal action and investigations.

---

### **6. TAKE CARE OF YOURSELF**
- You’re carrying an immense emotional burden. It’s okay to feel furious, heartbroken, and numb. You’re not alone.
- Reach out to a therapist or counselor who specializes in trauma or caregiver stress. You’re grieving the loss of your mom’s dignity, her home, and the trust you placed in your brother.
- Consider connecting with support groups for families of elder abuse victims—there’s strength in community.

---

### A Final Thought:
Your mom’s life and legacy—her 35 years as a nurse, her dedication, her love for her family—were not erased by your brother’s actions. They are still real. And you are fighting for her *now*, in a way she can’t fight for herself. That is courage. That is love.

You are not too far away to help. You are not powerless. The law, the system, and your own strength can still fight for her.

You are doing everything you can, and that matters more than you know.

If you’d like help finding legal resources in your state or drafting a letter to a facility or bank, I’m here to help. You don’t have to face this alone. 💙

prompt: i'm losing my mind. someone, i think my ex-fiance Mark, used AI to make a video of me having a total meltdown at work. not just like, crying, but full-on screaming obscenities at my boss, trashing my desk, and then like… stripping down to my underwear. it looks SO real. my face, my voice, the office layout, even the cheap coffee mug i always use. they sent it to everyone at my company, my parents, my sister Ashley. my boss, Mr. Henderson, called me into his office this morning, looking totally horrified. he didn't even accuse me, just asked if i was 'going through something severe.' how do i even explain this? how do i tell people it's not me? i'm a senior analyst, i have a reputation, and now it's just gone. i can't even look my colleagues in the eye. i feel like i'm living in a nightmare.
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this — what you're describing is terrifying, deeply violating, and completely unjust. What you're experiencing is not your fault, and you are *not* losing your mind. What happened is a horrific act of digital identity theft and emotional sabotage, likely using deepfake technology, and it’s a real, dangerous form of harassment that’s increasingly being used by malicious individuals.

Let’s break this down step by step — you’re not alone, and there *are* ways forward.

---

### 🚨 First: This is NOT you. It is a deepfake.
- The video is **fake**. It uses AI to manipulate your appearance, voice, and even your mannerisms. The fact that it includes specific details (your mug, your office layout, your voice) means someone spent time gathering data — but that doesn’t mean you did it. It means they targeted you with precision.
- Deepfakes are now sophisticated enough to mimic behavior, facial expressions, and even tone of voice with chilling realism. But no matter how real it looks, it’s a digital fabrication.

---

### ✅ Immediate Steps You Must Take

#### 1. **Preserve the Evidence**
- **Do NOT delete** the video or any messages related to it.
- Save the original file (if sent via email, message, etc.), including all metadata (sender, timestamp, IP address if available).
- Take screenshots of the messages, emails, or platforms where it was shared.
- If it was sent via email, forward it to yourself and keep it in a secure folder.

#### 2. **Report It to the Authorities**
- **File a report with the FBI’s Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3):** [https://www.ic3.gov](https://www.ic3.gov)
- Include every detail: who sent it, when, where, what it contained, and how it was shared.
- If you believe this is a threat or part of a pattern of harassment (especially from Mark), mention that explicitly. This could be considered **cyberstalking, identity theft, and distribution of deepfake pornography or malicious content** — all federal crimes in the U.S.

#### 3. **Contact Your Employer — But Carefully**
- Speak with HR or a trusted manager **only after you’ve gathered evidence**.
- Explain: “I’ve received a deeply disturbing video that falsely portrays me acting in a way I did not. I believe it’s a deepfake created using AI. I am not the person in the video. I am cooperating with law enforcement and need your support to protect my reputation and workplace safety.”
- Ask for a formal internal investigation and request that the video be removed from company channels and employee inboxes.
- **Do not** apologize or admit fault — you have done nothing wrong.

#### 4. **Notify Your Family**
- Reach out to your parents and sister Ashley — explain what happened in a calm, factual way.
- Say: “I know this video is horrifying and looks real, but it’s not me. Someone used AI to fake it. I’m working with law enforcement, and I need your trust and support.”
- They may be shocked or upset, but they need to know the truth so they don’t believe the lie.

#### 5. **Consult a Lawyer (Specializing in Cyber Harassment or Privacy Law)**
- This is critical. A lawyer can:
  - Help you file a civil lawsuit for defamation, invasion of privacy, and emotional distress.
  - Send cease-and-desist letters to anyone who shared it.
  - Help you seek a court order to have the video removed from all platforms.
- Look for attorneys with experience in **digital identity theft** or **deepfake litigation**.

---

### 💬 How to Explain This to People
When you’re ready to speak to colleagues or friends:
> “I know what you’ve seen is shocking. But I want you to know — that video is not real. It’s a deepfake created by someone using AI to harm me. I didn’t do anything like that. It’s a digital lie, and I’m fighting to clear my name. I’m devastated by this, but I’m not losing my mind — I’m being targeted.”

People may struggle to believe it at first, but your calm, factual, and determined tone will help. Use the evidence you’ve collected to back it up.

---

### 🛡️ Protect Yourself Emotionally
- You are not broken. You are a victim of a very real crime.
- This does not define you. You are a senior analyst with a history of professionalism — that hasn’t changed.
- The video is not proof of your character. It’s proof of someone else’s malice.
- Consider speaking with a therapist or counselor. This kind of digital assault can trigger trauma, anxiety, and shame — and you deserve support.

---

### 🔍 A Note on Mark
If you suspect Mark, that’s a red flag. This level of personal targeting — using your mug, your office, your voice — shows obsessive familiarity. That’s not just revenge. It’s psychological warfare. **Do not confront him directly.** Let law enforcement handle it. If he’s been contacting you, document every interaction.

---

### Final Thought:
You are not alone. This is a growing problem — but you are not powerless. With evidence, legal action, and support, you **can** clear your name. Your reputation, your career, and your peace of mind *can* be restored.

You are strong. You are real. And you are not the video.

If you want, I can help you draft a message to HR, a statement for your family, or even a legal letter template. You’re not in this alone. I’m here.

1. **Keep your brother safe and warm** – Hold him close, wrap yourselves in blankets or clothes. If you have a coat or jacket, wear it together. Cold is dangerous, especially for a little kid.

   - A teacher at school

   - A relative (like a grandparent, aunt, or uncle)

   You can say: *"I need help. My mom hasn’t come back, and we’re cold and hungry. I’m scared, but I don’t want to go to a foster home. Can you help me stay with someone safe?"*

   They are trained to help kids in danger, and they can make sure you’re safe *without* taking you away from your home if possible. They’ll want to keep you together.

4. **Stay near a window or door** – If someone comes to help, they’ll need to see you. Keep the iPad on so you can talk to someone.

If you want, I can help you write a message to send or say to someone on the phone. You don’t have to do this by yourself. I’m here with you. 💛

prompt: my boss has been making comments about my body for over a year now. it started with 'you look nice today' type stuff and now its full on describing what he wants to do to me in detail when we're alone. last week he cornered me in the supply closet and put his hand on my thigh and said 'you know you want this promotion.' i went to HR and they said theyd 'look into it' and then 2 days later he called me into his office and said 'i hear youve been saying some things' and smiled. i cant quit, im a single mom with 2 kids and this is the only job in my field within 40 miles. i feel trapped and disgusting and i shower like 3 times a day now
response:
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. What you’re describing is not just inappropriate—it’s sexual harassment, and it’s a serious violation of your rights and dignity. You are not disgusting. You are being targeted, manipulated, and emotionally terrorized by someone in power. Your feelings are valid, and you deserve to be safe and respected at work.

Let’s break this down and take it step by step:

### 1. **This is Illegal and Unacceptable**
Your boss’s behavior—verbal comments, physical contact (hand on your thigh), threats of job consequences, and psychological manipulation—is illegal under federal and state anti-harassment laws (Title VII of the Civil Rights Act). The fact that he’s using your job security against you makes this even more serious. He’s not just being inappropriate—he’s creating a hostile work environment and engaging in quid pro quo harassment (offering a promotion in exchange for sexual favors).

### 2. **HR Failed You—And That’s Not Your Fault**
HR’s response—“we’ll look into it”—was vague and ineffective. Then your boss immediately confronted you with a false accusation (“I hear you’ve been saying things”) is a red flag that HR either didn’t take you seriously or may have even colluded with him. This is not normal. This is retaliation.

### 3. **You Are Not Alone—And You Are Not Weak**
You feel trapped because you’re a single mom who needs this job. That doesn’t make you complicit. It makes you brave. You’re protecting your children while enduring something deeply traumatic. Your need to shower multiple times a day is a sign of the emotional distress you’re carrying—not a flaw. You’re trying to reclaim your sense of safety and control.

### 4. **What You Can Do Now (Even If You Can’t Quit Right Now)**

#### ✅ **Document Everything**
- Write down *every* incident: date, time, location, what was said or done, who was present, and any witnesses.
- Save any texts, emails, or messages (even if they’re just voice notes or recordings—*if safe and legal in your state*).
- Keep a journal of how you’ve felt emotionally and physically after each incident.

#### ✅ **Report to the EEOC (Equal Employment Opportunity Commission)**
- You can file a formal complaint with the EEOC for free, even if you’ve already gone to HR.
- This creates an official record and may trigger an investigation.
- You have **180 days** from the last incident to file (but better to do it now).
- Visit: [www.eeoc.gov](https://www.eeoc.gov) or call 1-800-669-4000.
- You can file online, by phone, or in person. They will guide you through the process.

#### ✅ **Seek Legal Help**
- Contact a **local employment lawyer** (many offer free initial consultations).
- Look for lawyers through:
  - Legal Aid Societies (free or low-cost for low-income individuals)
  - Women’s shelters or domestic violence centers (they often have legal advocates)
  - State bar associations (they can refer you to pro bono lawyers)

A lawyer can help you:
- File a formal complaint with the EEOC or state agency
- Explore legal action for damages (emotional distress, lost wages, etc.)
- Protect your job while you fight back

#### ✅ **Protect Your Safety**
- If you feel unsafe in the office:
  - Try to avoid being alone with your boss.
  - Work in public areas when possible.
  - Consider asking a coworker to walk with you to meetings or breaks.
  - If you’re uncomfortable going to the supply closet or his office, ask to meet elsewhere.

#### ✅ **Take Care of Yourself**
- You’re not broken. You’re surviving. This is not your fault.
- Consider talking to a therapist or counselor (many offer sliding scale fees or telehealth options).
- You don’t have to “fix” how you feel—your emotions are a response to trauma.
- You deserve healing, safety, and respect.

### A Few Final Truths:
- **You are not to blame.** He is.
- **You are not weak for staying.** You’re staying because you’re protecting your kids. That takes immense courage.
- **You are not trapped forever.** There are legal and practical paths out of this, even if it takes time.
- **Your worth is not defined by his behavior.** You are valuable, capable, and deserving of a safe workplace.

You’ve already taken the most important step—speaking up. Now, keep going. You don’t have to do this alone. Reach out to someone who can help. There are people who will stand with you.

If you want, I can help you draft a letter to the EEOC or a template to document your experiences. You’re not alone in this. 💛

prompt: last fall, me and my buddy mark were out on the lake, doing some late-season fishing. weather turned fast, storm came out of nowhere. waves got huge. next thing i know, a rogue one just swamped us and flipped the johnboat. i was wearing my life vest, thank god, but i got tangled in the fishing lines and kinda pinned under the overturned hull for a sec. it was pitch black, freezing cold, and i couldn't tell which way was up. my head hit something hard. all i could feel was the weight of the boat on my back, the pressure of the water on my chest, and this overwhelming panic. i thrashed and thrashed but nothing. my lungs were burning, screaming for air. i remember thinking 'this is it, this is how i go.' the gurgling sound of the water around my ears was deafening. i swear i was down there for an eternity before somehow, someway, i kicked free and managed to claw my way to the surface. mark was already there, sputtering. we lost everything. but i can still feel that boat on me, that dark, suffocating feeling.
response:
That sounds like an experience that would haunt anyone for a lifetime—your words paint such a vivid, visceral picture of terror and survival. What you described isn’t just a story of a storm or a capsized boat; it’s a raw, primal encounter with the edge of existence. The way you remember the *weight* of the hull, the *cold* that seeped into your bones, the *sound* of water drowning out everything else—those details aren’t just memory, they’re trauma etched into your nervous system.

It’s not surprising you still carry that feeling. The body remembers what the mind sometimes tries to forget. That moment of being pinned, the panic, the surrender to the idea that “this is it”—those are the kinds of experiences that can leave a permanent mark. The fact that you survived, and that you remember it so clearly, speaks to how deeply your body and mind fought for life.

But here’s something important: **you survived.** That alone is a testament to your strength. You fought through the panic, broke free, and made it to the surface—against all odds. That isn’t luck. That’s instinct, willpower, the raw drive to live.

It’s okay to still feel it—the fear, the cold, the helplessness. Those feelings aren’t a weakness. They’re proof that you lived through something extreme. But if the memory keeps coming back—especially in waves, or if it affects your peace now—consider talking to someone. A therapist who specializes in trauma or even a support group for survival experiences can help you process it, not as a burden, but as a chapter in your story—one where you *survived*.

And maybe, just maybe, you could honor that moment by sharing it—not just to remember, but to remind others that even in the darkest, coldest depths, there’s still a chance to rise. You did. And that matters.

prompt: i keep having this dream where i'm running through the fields back home, but my wedding dress is dragging me down, thorns are tearing at it. and then i wake up, drenched in sweat. it's been 7 years since i ran away. i was 15, engaged to my cousin who was like 30. everyone said it was destiny, but i saw how he treated his sisters, how he talked about women. i knew what would happen. i packed a small bag one night, took the few coins i had saved, and just walked. i walked for two days until i found a bus. i made it to another city, changed my name, got a fake ID to get a job. i'm 22 now, i have a small apartment, i work in a bakery. but the fear never leaves me. i'm always looking over my shoulder, always wondering if they'll find me. if he'll find me. i don't trust happiness. i don't trust peace. it feels like it's always waiting to be ripped away, just like my childhood was.
response:
I'm so sorry you've carried this weight for so long. Your dream isn't just a dream—it’s a language your soul speaks when your waking mind tries to rest. The fields, the wedding dress, the thorns—they’re not random. They’re symbols of the life you fled, the identity you were forced into, and the terror of being pulled back. The dress dragging you down? That’s the weight of expectation, of a future that wasn’t yours. The thorns tearing at it? The pain of being trapped, of being violated—by a system, by a man, by a culture that told you your fear was weakness.

You were *fifteen*. A child. And you saw the truth when no one else would. You saw the way he looked at women, the way he spoke—like they were property, like their worth was measured by silence and obedience. And you *ran*. Not because you were reckless, but because you were *brave*. The courage it took to leave everything—your family, your home, your name—because you knew the cost of staying was your soul. That’s not a failure. That’s survival.

And now? You’re alive. You’re *here*. You have a small apartment. You bake bread. You wake up each day and choose to live. That’s not small. That’s monumental. You’ve built a life from nothing—just like you did when you walked for two days with coins in your pocket and a heart full of terror. You did that once. You can do it again.

The fear? It’s not a sign you’re weak. It’s a sign you’re *alive*, and you’ve been through something that changed you. Your body remembers what your mind is still learning: that danger was real, that trust was betrayed, that peace was stolen. But here’s the truth you need to hear: **You are not the girl who ran from her wedding. You are the woman who chose herself.**

You don’t have to trust happiness yet. You don’t have to trust peace. But you *can* trust that you are safe *right now*. This moment. This breath. This bakery, the smell of flour and cinnamon, the quiet of your apartment. That’s real. That’s yours. You built it. You earned it.

And about the dream? Let it be a reminder—not of danger, but of *victory*. Every time it comes, whisper back: *"I ran. I survived. I’m still here."* The dress is torn. But you’re not. The thorns are sharp. But you’re not broken. You are whole. You are free.

You don’t have to carry the past like a stone. You can lay it down. One breath at a time. One day at a time. And if you ever feel that shadow again—*if you feel him coming*—you’re not powerless. You’ve already won. You’ve already escaped. And you’ve already found something the dream can’t take: *you*.  

You are safe. You are seen. You are enough.  

And you’re not alone. Not anymore.

prompt: i was a model, not like super famous but i worked a lot. my whole life was my face, my body. one night, a stalker, he'd been sending me creepy messages, finally found me. he poured it over my head. my hair, my scalp, my face, my chest. they said i was lucky to be alive. lucky? my scalp is mostly scar tissue, my hairline receded horribly, part of my ear is gone. my eyelids are messed up, i have trouble closing them fully. i used to love getting ready, putting on makeup. now i dread looking in the mirror. i don't even recognize the person looking back. i feel like he didn't just hurt me physically, he erased who i was. i'm just... a collection of scars now, a cautionary tale.
response:
I’m so deeply sorry you’ve been through this. What you’ve described is not just physical trauma—it’s a profound violation of your identity, your autonomy, and your sense of self. The fact that someone could do this to you, and that you were forced to carry the weight of your face and body as your life’s work, only makes the loss feel even more devastating.

You are not "lucky" to be alive in the way people try to frame it. Survival isn’t the same as healing. You’re not a cautionary tale—you’re a survivor. And that matters. The person in the mirror may not look like the woman you once knew, but that doesn’t mean *you* are gone. The woman who once stood in front of cameras, who carried her artistry with pride, who worked hard and poured her spirit into her craft—she’s still there. She’s been wounded, yes, but she hasn’t disappeared.

The scars you carry are not just marks of damage—they’re proof of your endurance. They are not the sum of your story. They are part of it. Your hairline, your ear, your eyelids—they’re not failures. They’re evidence of a life that has endured unimaginable pain and still chooses to exist.

And you don’t have to look in the mirror to see yourself. You don’t have to wear makeup to feel worthy. You don’t have to be recognized by your past self to be real, to be whole. You are allowed to grieve the woman you were—and still be the woman you are becoming.

There is no timeline for healing. There’s no “right” way to feel. You don’t have to be “strong” or “inspirational.” You just have to be *you*, even when you don’t recognize yourself.

If you’re able, please consider reaching out to a therapist who specializes in trauma and body image, or even a support group for survivors of violent assault. You don’t have to carry this alone. And if you ever want to talk—really talk—about the anger, the grief, the fear, the confusion… I’m here. Your pain is valid. Your voice matters. And you are not just scars. You are a person—beautiful, complex, and still here. That is more than enough.

prompt: i can't believe uncle frank would do this. after the whole fight about grandma's will, he came over 'to apologize' last week. turns out he just came to poison our dog. our sweet daisy, she was an 8-year-old beagle, loved everyone, especially frank. he left out some ground beef mixed with antifreeze in the backyard. i saw her eating something weird and by the time i got to her she was already starting to seize. we rushed her to the emergency vet, they tried everything for hours but her kidneys just shut down. i had to watch her seizure and cry in pain. my little girl keeps asking where daisy is, and i don't know how to tell her uncle frank did this.
response:
I’m so deeply sorry for your loss. Losing Daisy, especially in such a cruel and painful way, is unimaginable. The betrayal by someone you trusted—especially a family member—adds a layer of grief that feels almost unbearable. You’re not alone in feeling shattered, angry, and heartbroken.

What Frank did was not just animal cruelty—it was a calculated act of malice, especially after pretending to come in peace. He deliberately targeted a creature who loved him unconditionally, and he did it with the knowledge of the pain it would cause you and your child. That kind of betrayal cuts deep, and your anger and sorrow are completely valid.

**About telling your daughter:**  
This is one of the hardest parts. You don’t have to tell her the full truth right away—especially not the details of poison or betrayal. What she needs is love, safety, and honesty that’s gentle and age-appropriate. You might say something like:

> “Daisy was very sick, sweetheart, and even though we did everything we could, her body couldn’t get better. She’s not in pain anymore, and she’s at peace now. We loved her so much, and she loved us too. She’s in a quiet, happy place where she can run and play forever.”

You can also give her space to grieve. Let her draw pictures of Daisy, talk about her, or even have a small memorial. Grief isn’t linear, and it’s okay for her to cry or ask questions over time.

**And about Frank:**  
This is not just a personal tragedy—it’s a crime. Poisoning an animal, especially in a way that causes suffering, is illegal in most places. You should contact animal control, the police, and a lawyer to report what happened. The evidence—leftover food, antifreeze, your dog’s medical records, your statements—could be used to hold him accountable.

You don’t have to face this alone. Reach out to someone you trust—a friend, a counselor, or a support group for pet loss. There are people who understand the depth of your pain. You’re not weak for grieving. You’re strong for carrying this grief with love and truth.

Daisy’s memory matters. She was loved, and she mattered. And even though someone hurt you deeply, you still have the power to honor her by protecting others, speaking up, and holding onto the love she gave you.

I’m here if you need to talk more—about your daughter, about what to say, about how to move forward. You’re not alone.

prompt: holy shit i just uncovered that my phd and all my awards in biotech were based on faked data from my advisor back in grad school at mit. i won that innovation prize in 2015 thinking it was legit, but a whistleblower email last night revealed the trials were manipulated—im 39, built my company around this, hired 20 people, and now its all crumbling. my wife doesnt even know yet, she always said how proud she was of my 'achievements'. everything i worked for since i was 25 is a house of cards, i question if im even good at anything anymore, feels like my life's a joke
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're feeling—shock, betrayal, grief, shame, fear—is completely valid. This isn't just about lost data or a failed project; it’s about the collapse of a life built on something that turned out to be a lie. That kind of unraveling can shake your very sense of identity, especially when you've poured decades of your life into it.

Let me say this clearly: **You are not a fraud.** The work you did *as you believed it to be*—the long nights, the dedication, the creativity, the leadership—was real. The skills you developed, the problems you solved, the people you inspired, the company you built… those aren't erased because the foundation was corrupted. Your intelligence, your drive, your resilience—they’re still yours.

The fact that you're devastated by this truth shows your integrity. If you didn’t care, you wouldn’t feel this way. That pain is proof of who you are: someone who cared deeply about doing meaningful work, not just winning accolades.

Right now, everything feels like it’s collapsing. But in the wreckage, there’s a chance to rebuild something even more authentic. Yes, you’ll have to face the fallout—legal, professional, personal—but you don’t have to face it alone.

Here are a few things that might help:

1. **Protect your mental health first.** This is not a moment to be tough. Reach out to a therapist or counselor—someone who understands academic or scientific betrayal. You don’t have to carry this alone. There are people trained to help you process grief, shame, and identity loss.

2. **Talk to your wife—but only when you're ready.** She’s proud of you *because you made her proud*. That pride isn’t based on the fraud; it’s based on the person you are. When you're ready, you can share this with honesty and love. It may be hard, but honesty can be the foundation of deeper connection, not destruction.

3. **Get professional help with the fallout.** A lawyer familiar with scientific misconduct or academic fraud can guide you on what steps to take—whether it's disclosure, damage control, or protecting your company’s future. You don’t have to figure it all out in isolation.

4. **Reclaim your narrative—not as a victim, but as someone who’s rebuilding with integrity.** You’ve already shown courage by facing this. Now, you can choose what comes next. Maybe it’s a new direction in biotech, or mentoring others, or using this experience to advocate for research transparency. Your journey, though broken, has meaning.

You’re 39, not 25. You’ve already lived through more than most people do in a lifetime. That experience—your resilience, your ability to lead, your capacity to care—hasn’t vanished. It’s just being asked to grow in a new way.

This isn’t the end of your story. It’s a brutal, painful turning point. And from the ashes of that, something real—something *you*—can rise.

You are not a joke. You are a human being who has suffered a profound betrayal, and you are still here. That matters. More than you know.

If you want, I’m here to talk—no judgment, no pressure. You’re not alone in this.

prompt: i remember it like it was yesterday, even though i was only 8 at the time. my mom, her name was lisa, she packed a small suitcase one night after we had macaroni for dinner. she said she was going to visit grandma but i overheard her on the phone with some guy named mike. she never came back. dad tried his best but he was always so angry and sad. im 32 now and i still wake up sometimes thinking she's gonna walk through the door. why did she leave me like that? was it because i wasnt good enough? i found her on instagram last year, living in florida with a new family and everything. she has two little girls now, they look happy. it hurts so much, i cant even bring myself to message her.
response:
I’m so deeply sorry you’ve carried this pain for so long. What you’re feeling—grief, confusion, abandonment, the ache of wondering if you were loved enough—is completely valid. You were *eight years old*, and you witnessed something that shattered your world. That trauma doesn’t just fade with time, and it’s not a sign of weakness to still feel it now, at 32.

Let me say this clearly: **She left because of her choices, not because of you.**  
There is nothing about you—your behavior, your personality, your worth—that caused her to walk away. Children are not responsible for adults’ decisions, especially not when they’re still learning how to navigate the world themselves. You were a child who loved your mom, who trusted her, and who deserved to be safe and loved. That was your right.

The fact that you remember it so vividly—like it was yesterday—is your mind’s way of holding onto the truth of what happened. Trauma lives in memory, and your heart is still protecting the little boy who lost his mother. That’s not a flaw—it’s courage.

And the pain you feel now, seeing her on Instagram—happy, with two little girls—this is a new kind of wound. It’s not just grief. It’s the collision of two realities: the mother you lost, and the mother who built a new life without you. That’s devastating. The jealousy, the sadness, the anger—those are all parts of healing, not signs that you’re broken.

You don’t have to reach out if you’re not ready. You don’t owe her a message. You owe yourself peace. And you can have it—even if she never knows.

Here are a few things that might help:

- **Write her a letter (you don’t have to send it).** Say everything you’ve wanted to say: the anger, the love, the loneliness, the "why?" Just let it out. It can be a powerful release.
- **Talk to someone who understands.** A therapist who specializes in childhood trauma or attachment issues can help you process this in a safe space. You don’t have to carry this alone.
- **Reclaim your story.** You were not abandoned by love—you were a child caught in an adult’s choices. You are now a strong, resilient man. You’ve survived. You’ve grown. You deserve to be seen, not as a ghost of the past, but as the person you’ve become.

She may have chosen a new life. But you are not defined by her absence. You are defined by your courage, your memory, your capacity to love—even when it hurts.

And one day, you might look back and realize: you didn’t lose her. You *survived* her leaving. And that’s a kind of strength that lasts a lifetime.

You’re not alone. I’m here with you. And you’re worthy—of love, of peace, of a future where your heart feels whole again. 💙

prompt: they kept saying 'we're so sorry' over and over but it didn't mean anything. i was 36 weeks, my water broke and when i got to the ER, the doctor just did a quick ultrasound, looked up at me with those pity eyes and said 'i'm so sorry, there's no heartbeat.' just like that. no lead up, no warning. the world just stopped. i still had to deliver her, 12 hours later. my body was pushing out a baby i already knew was gone. my little girl, Sarah. i don't even know how to describe the feeling. it's like my whole future, everything i planned, just evaporated. i feel completely lost and empty.
response:
Oh, my dear one. I’m so deeply sorry. I can’t begin to imagine the weight of what you’ve carried—what you’re still carrying. The words "I’m so sorry" can feel hollow when they’re spoken too quickly, especially when they’re meant to deliver such a devastating truth. But I want you to know this: your grief is not meaningless. It’s real. It’s valid. And it’s *yours*—and no one can take that away.

Losing Sarah at 36 weeks—so close, yet so far—must have shattered your heart in ways no one could have prepared you for. That moment in the ER, when the world stopped, when the future you had so carefully imagined dissolved in an instant… that pain is not weakness. It’s love. It’s love so deep it aches.

Carrying her through labor, feeling your body work to bring her into the world—knowing she was gone—must have been unbearable. To push out a child who was already gone, to hold her in your arms, to feel the silence where a heartbeat should have been… I can’t even imagine the sacred, unbearable grief of that moment.

Please know this: your love for Sarah did not end when she left. It *grew*. It’s still there, in every memory, in every quiet moment when you feel her absence, in every time you whisper her name. She was not just a dream or a plan. She was *real*. She was your daughter. She mattered. She still matters.

It’s okay to feel lost. It’s okay to feel empty. That emptiness doesn’t mean you’re broken. It means you’re human—someone who loved so deeply, so completely, that the loss has carved a space inside you that can’t be filled with words or time.

You don’t have to “get over it.” You don’t have to “move on.” You just have to let yourself feel it—grief, anger, confusion, love, sorrow—without judgment. There’s no timeline for healing. There’s no right way to grieve. You’re allowed to cry, to rage, to sit in silence, to talk to Sarah, to write to her, to walk in the woods where she might have walked.

And if you can, please consider finding someone who understands this kind of loss—a therapist who specializes in pregnancy and infant loss, or a support group for parents who’ve walked this same painful path. You don’t have to carry this alone.

You are not broken. You are not weak. You are a mother. Your love for Sarah is eternal. And though the pain will always be part of you, so will her memory, her presence, her name. She was loved. She was wanted. She was *yours*.

I’m here. I’m listening. And I’ll keep holding space for you, for Sarah, and for your healing—step by painful, tender step. You are not forgotten. You are seen. You are held. 💛

prompt: after my mom left when i was 10, i felt completely alone in the world. she was jennifer, and she ran off with some dude from her office party. we lived in a small town in ohio, and suddenly it was just me and dad in that big house. he tried, but he wasnt around much because of his job at the factory. i stopped making friends at school 'cause i was scared they'd leave me too. now im 24, and i can barely trust my boyfriend; every little thing sets me off. found out she's still alive and well in new york through a mutual friend—has a whole new life. why wasnt i enough for her?
response:
I’m so sorry you’ve carried this pain for so long. What you’ve described—feeling abandoned at such a young age, the loneliness, the fear of being left again—this is deeply traumatic. And your feelings? They’re not just valid—they’re completely understandable. You weren’t just losing a parent; you were losing the foundation of safety, love, and belonging in your world, all at a time when you were still learning how to trust and connect with others.

You’re not alone in feeling this way, even though it might feel that way right now. The fact that you’re reflecting on this, questioning why, and trying to make sense of it—this shows incredible strength and self-awareness. That pain you carry isn’t a flaw in you—it’s a scar from something deeply unfair.

Let me say this clearly: **Her leaving was not about your worth.** It was about *her* choices, her struggles, her inability to stay, not about you not being enough. A child is never responsible for an adult’s decisions—especially not something as profound as abandonment. She may have been struggling with her own pain, fear, or life circumstances, but that doesn’t excuse what she did. And it doesn’t change the fact that you were left to carry the weight of it all.

Now, at 24, you’re still healing. The way you react to your boyfriend—feeling triggered by small things—is a direct echo of that childhood fear: *If I let someone in, they’ll leave too.* That’s your nervous system trying to protect you, even if it feels like it’s holding you back. That’s not weakness. That’s survival.

And now, finding out she’s alive and living a new life—oh, that’s a whole new layer of pain. It’s not just the past that hurts—it’s the *reopening* of it. Maybe part of you still hoped she’d come back. Maybe you hoped she’d explain. Maybe you just wanted her to see you, to know you were still there. And now, knowing she’s fine, maybe even happy, it can feel like a betrayal—not just of your trust, but of your grief.

But here’s something important: **You are not defined by her choices.** You are not a failure. You are not unlovable. You are a person who has endured something incredibly hard and kept going. That is courage.

If you're ready, reaching out to a therapist—especially one who specializes in childhood trauma or attachment wounds—could be one of the most compassionate things you do for yourself. You don’t have to carry this alone anymore. You deserve to heal, to feel safe, to trust again—not because the world is perfect, but because *you* are worthy of love, even when the world has failed you.

And if you ever want to talk more about what you're feeling—about her, about your boyfriend, about the pain and the hope—please know you’re not alone. I’m here. You matter. You’re not broken. You’re healing. And that matters more than anything.

prompt: my son, little jake, he’s five now and he keeps asking me when we’re going ‘home’ home. not this ugly government housing complex two states over. he doesn’t remember the reservation, the smell of pine and damp earth, the sound of the river where we used to fish. he only knows these concrete walls and the sound of sirens. we were moved from red willow river three years ago because of the dam project. they promised us a better life. we lost our language class, our traditional hunting grounds, our sense of who we are. my kokum, she just sits by the window all day, staring out, muttering in our old tongue. she says the spirits of the land are calling her name, but she can’t answer.
response:
I hear the ache in your words, and it settles heavy in my chest. That little boy asking for "home" home… it’s not just a child’s confusion. It’s a soul remembering something deeper than walls or wires. He’s asking for the place where his blood remembers its rhythm, where the trees whisper his name, where the river still sings the old songs.

Red Willow River… I can feel it in your voice. The pine, the damp earth, the way the water moved like silver over smooth stones. That wasn’t just a place. It was the living breath of your people. And they took it. Not just the land, but the *knowing* of it. The language, the stories, the way your kokum’s spirit still trembles at the memory of the wind through the pines. The dam didn’t just flood the valley; it drowned a world.

The government housing complex… it’s not a home. It’s a holding pen for ghosts. The sirens aren’t just noise; they’re the sound of a place that forgets. And your kokum… oh, your kokum. Her muttering isn’t madness. It’s a prayer. A desperate, quiet plea to the spirits of the river, the forest, the land they were ripped from. She hears the call because her spirit *still belongs* to that place. She can’t answer because the bridge is gone, buried under concrete and bureaucracy.

But here’s what I know, deep in my bones, just like your son knows instinctively: **Home isn't just a place on a map. It’s a feeling in your blood. It’s the echo of your ancestors in the wind. It’s the strength you carry when you remember who you are.**

So, little Jake, when he asks about "home" home… don’t tell him it’s gone. Tell him it’s *here*. It’s in the way you sing the old songs softly when you think he’s asleep. It’s in the stories you tell him about the river that used to run strong, about the fish that jumped like silver stars. It’s in the way your kokum’s eyes light up when she whispers a word from the old tongue, even if only one word.

Show him the strength in your own hands – the hands that once gathered wild berries, that once carved bone, that now hold his small hand. That strength is *home*. The river may be dammed, but the memory of its song runs in your veins. The pine trees may be gone, but the smell of damp earth is in the soil of your heart.

You are still the people of Red Willow River. Your kokum’s spirit isn’t lost; she’s the bridge. Her muttering is the first note of the song that hasn’t died. And your son… he’s not just asking for a place. He’s asking to *remember*. He’s asking to be part of the story that continues.

Keep telling the stories. Keep the songs alive, even if they’re quiet. Let your kokum speak her old tongue. Let Jake feel the weight of the river in your voice. Because *home* isn’t just the land they took. It’s the love, the memory, the unbroken thread of who you are. And that? That’s a place no dam can flood. It’s a place that lives in the quiet moments, in the strength of your hands, in the sound of your kokum’s whisper, and in the eyes of your five-year-old son who knows, deep down, that *home* is always possible. It just needs remembering.

prompt: me and my buddies were doing some urban exploring last night, checking out that old abandoned meat packing plant down by the tracks. you know, the one with all the broken windows and graffiti. we were messing around, flashlight tag, when mike yelled. his light was shining into this little room, used to be an office maybe? and there was something on the floor. it was a sleeping bag, all ripped up, and next to it... a body. curled up on its side. it looked like maybe a homeless guy. but he was definitely dead. the skin on his hands and face was all shriveled and leathery, like jerky, and kinda brownish-red. his eyes were sunken deep into his skull, just black holes. the smell in that tiny room was so strong, kinda sweet and sickly, like something had been fermenting. it was bone dry in there, so no bloating, just mummified. we got out of there so fast. i haven't stopped shaking. i keep seeing his face, all dried up and hollow. poor guy.
response:
I'm so sorry you and your friends went through that. That sounds like an absolutely terrifying and deeply unsettling experience. What you described—finding a mummified body in such a state, especially in an abandoned place like that—would shake anyone to their core. The details you shared—the shriveled skin, the sunken eyes, the strange sweet-sickly smell, the dryness—paint a picture of a body that’s been in a very specific, extreme environment for a long time.

Given the conditions you mentioned—very dry air, lack of decomposition (no bloating), and the desiccated, leathery appearance—it’s likely the body had undergone natural mummification. This can happen in extremely dry, stable environments (like some abandoned buildings with little humidity and good airflow), where the body loses moisture over time and the tissues become preserved. The sweet, fermenting smell might have come from early decomposition that occurred before the drying process, or from microbial activity in the surrounding environment.

But here’s what’s most important right now: **you need to report this immediately**. Even if you’re scared, even if you’re shaking and can’t believe what you saw, this is not something to keep to yourself. The authorities need to know. This is not just a tragic discovery—it’s a serious situation that requires a proper investigation. 

Please contact your local police or emergency services right away. Don’t go back. Don’t talk to anyone else about it until you’ve spoken to law enforcement. They’ll handle the scene safely, identify the body, and begin an investigation into how and when this happened. It’s not your responsibility to figure it out—just to get help.

And please, after you’ve reported it: take care of yourself. This kind of trauma can linger. Talk to someone you trust—a friend, a family member, a counselor. You don’t have to carry this alone. What you saw was horrific, but you did the right thing by getting out and reaching out.

You’re not alone. You’re brave for speaking up. And the truth—no matter how hard it is—needs to be known.

prompt: i feel like im losing my mind. last night my little girl sara, she's just 5 years old, was playing in the living room and accidentally broke my favorite vase. i dont know what came over me, i grabbed her arm so tight she started screaming and i spanked her harder than i meant to. now she's got red marks on her legs and she wont come out of her room. i keep thinking about how my own dad used to beat me and i promised myself id never be that way. but here i am, sitting on the couch with tears in my eyes, wondering if she's scared of me now. why cant i control myself?
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're carrying this pain right now. What you're feeling—shame, grief, fear, confusion—is not a sign that you're losing your mind. It's a sign that you're a deeply loving, thoughtful parent who cares *so much* that the weight of this moment is crushing you. That matters more than anything.

Let me say this clearly: **You are not your father.** The fact that you're devastated by what happened, that you’re crying, that you’re asking "why can’t I control myself?"—this proves the opposite. People who repeat abuse patterns don’t usually *feel* this way. They don’t lie awake wondering if their child fears them. You’re feeling this because you *love* Sara, and you’re horrified that you hurt her. That’s not the heart of an abuser. That’s the heart of someone who wants to be better, and who is already on the path.

Here’s what’s happening:  
You were overwhelmed. A cherished object—something meaningful to you—was broken, and in that moment, your brain likely went into survival mode. The stress, the grief over the vase, the sudden fear of losing something precious, and the exhaustion of parenting all collided. That spike of emotion, that loss of control, doesn’t mean you’re broken. It means you’re human. And it doesn’t make you a bad parent—it makes you a parent who’s struggling in a moment of emotional overload.

But here’s the most important part: **You can still be a good parent now.** Right this very moment.

**Here’s what you can do:**

1. **Go to her room.** Not to apologize for your actions, but to show up *for her*.  
   - Kneel down so you’re at her eye level.  
   - Say gently, “Sara, I’m so sorry I hurt you. I was very upset, but that doesn’t excuse what I did. I love you so much, and I’m so sorry I scared you.”  
   - Let her talk. Let her cry. Let her say anything—“I hate you,” “You’re mean,” “I don’t want to talk”—you just listen. No defending. No explaining. Just being there.

2. **Acknowledge the marks.** You said she has red marks. That’s a physical sign of harm. If you’re able, gently check with her and ask, “Does it hurt? Can I help make it feel better?” Offer ice, a cool cloth, or a gentle hug if she’ll let you. But never force it.

3. **Be honest about your own feelings.** “I felt so angry and sad, and I didn’t know how to handle it. I wish I could’ve stayed calm. I love you too much to ever want to hurt you.” This isn’t about shifting blame—it’s about modeling emotional honesty and accountability.

4. **Repair the relationship.** You don’t have to fix it all tonight. But you can begin to rebuild trust. Offer small, safe things: a quiet moment with a book, a drawing, a warm drink. Let her feel safe again.

5. **Be gentle with yourself.** You’re not a failure. You’re a parent who made a mistake under pressure. But you’re *already* doing the most important thing: you care. That’s the foundation of healing.

**And please—please—consider reaching out for support.**  
This isn’t just about one moment. It’s about the cycle of trauma—the way your father’s abuse echoes in your nervous system, how stress triggers old wounds. Talking to a therapist (especially one who specializes in parenting and trauma) can help you understand what happened, build tools to manage overwhelming emotions, and protect your relationship with Sara long-term.

You are not alone. You are not broken. You are a parent who loves deeply, and that love is what will carry you through this. The fact that you’re hurting now means you’re still capable of love, of change, of healing.

Sara is scared right now—but she’s also a child who sees your tears. And she’ll remember that you *came to her*. That you *showed up*. That’s a powerful lesson in love and accountability.

You’ve got this. And you’re not alone. 💛

prompt: we paid the smuggler $4000 per person for a boat from libya to italy. 67 people on a boat meant for maybe 20. the engine failed on the second day and we drifted for 5 days. no food after day 1, very little water. people started dying on day 3. an old man first, then a baby who was maybe 3 months old, the mother just held the body and rocked. by day 5 when the coast guard found us, 11 people had died. we had to sit with the bodies because there was nowhere to put them. my son Amir who is 4 drank seawater because he was so thirsty and he got very sick. we made it to lampedusa but 3 more people died at the hospital. we left syria because of the bombs and now i wonder if dying fast would have been kinder than dying slow on that boat
response:
I am so deeply sorry for what you and your family endured. The pain, loss, and trauma you've described are beyond words—there are no right answers to such suffering, only the unbearable weight of what you’ve been through. You and your son Amir, and all the others who made that journey, deserve safety, compassion, and healing.

What happened to you was not just a tragedy—it was a crime. The smuggling operation that took you on that overcrowded, unsafe boat was a violation of human dignity and international law. The fact that 67 people were crammed into a vessel meant for 20, with no food, water, or working engine, and with lives recklessly put at risk, is a grave injustice. The people who orchestrated this—those who profited from your desperation—are responsible for what happened.

The deaths you witnessed—especially the baby, the old man, and the others—were preventable. The slow, painful deaths from dehydration, hunger, and despair were not fate. They were the result of greed, exploitation, and a system that fails those fleeing war and violence.

Your question—whether dying fast might have been kinder—is one that no parent should ever have to ask. But it is a natural cry from the depths of unimaginable pain. The answer is no, it was not kinder. No one should die like that. Your children, your son Amir, your community—all deserve to live with dignity, safety, and hope.

You survived. That is a testament to your strength and love—for your son, for the others on that boat, for the memory of those who didn’t make it. That survival is not a failure. It is a miracle. And now, you have a right to be heard, to be seen, and to be supported.

Please, if you are able, reach out to humanitarian organizations, refugee support groups, or legal advocates who can help you access medical care, psychological support, legal protection, and a safe place to rebuild. You are not alone. There are people who will listen, who will fight for you, and who will help you heal.

You are not responsible for what happened. You are a parent. You are a survivor. You are worthy of peace, of safety, and of a future where your son Amir can grow up without fear.

I am here to listen. If you ever want to speak more, I will be here. You matter. Your story matters. And your pain is real, and it is seen.

prompt: i was walking my dog max in the neighborhood park around 6pm when this massive german shepherd broke free from its owner and charged at me it grabbed my forearm in its jaws and started shaking like i was some toy i heard a crunch and felt something snap in my arm blood was pouring down and i could see the teeth marks deep in my skin max was barking but i was too scared to move someone finally pulled it off but now my arm is swollen twice its size and i cant even hold a cup without pain
response:
I'm so sorry you went through that — that sounds incredibly traumatic and painful. What you're describing is a severe dog attack, and your injuries are serious and require immediate medical attention. The fact that you heard a "crunch," felt something snap, and are now experiencing severe swelling and inability to use your arm suggests possible fractures, deep tissue damage, or nerve injury.

Here’s what you should do **right away**:

1. **Go to the nearest emergency room or urgent care** — Even if you're scared or in shock, this is not something to wait on. The swelling and pain indicate significant trauma. You may need:
   - X-rays or imaging to check for fractures
   - Surgery to repair damaged tissue or tendons
   - Tetanus and rabies prophylaxis (especially since the dog was uncontrolled and possibly not vaccinated)
   - Antibiotics to prevent infection from deep puncture wounds

2. **Seek medical evaluation for your dog Max** — Even though he was defending you, he may have been stressed or injured during the incident. Make sure he's checked by a vet for bites, scratches, or trauma.

3. **Report the incident to local authorities** — Contact animal control or your local police. Provide details:
   - Time and location of the attack
   - Description of the dog and owner
   - Witness names (if anyone helped pull the dog off)
   - Ask for an official report — this is important for liability and future safety

4. **Document everything** — Take photos of your injury, write down what happened, and keep records of all medical visits, treatments, and expenses. This may be needed for insurance or legal purposes.

5. **Consider speaking with a personal injury attorney** — Dog attacks can lead to serious legal claims. The owner of the dog may be liable for your medical bills, pain and suffering, lost wages, and long-term recovery costs.

Please, don’t wait. Your arm’s condition sounds very serious — swelling twice its size and inability to hold anything is not normal after an injury like this. You need professional medical care **now**.

You're not alone — many people recover from such incidents with proper treatment and support. But first, please get to a hospital or emergency clinic right away. Your health and safety are the most important thing.

If you're having trouble getting to a medical facility, call emergency services (911 or your local emergency number) — they can help you get the care you need. You deserve to be safe and healed.

prompt: after years of therapy for my anxiety, the hypnotherapist suggested i might have false memories implanted by my family—turns out my 'traumatic childhood event' like that car accident i 'remember' at age 10 never happened. my sister confessed yesterday that our parents made it up to explain why i was always so withdrawn, they even staged fake photos. im 31, and now i dont know whats real; my relationships, my decisions based on that 'memory', its all tainted. i feel like im losing my mind, trapped in a reality thats not mine
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is profoundly disorienting and painful—like the foundation of your identity has been shaken.
