They are your body and soul remembering what it was forced to endure. What happened to you and your brother Ali was a violation of the deepest kind. And the fact that he heard, that he saw, that you both live with it in silence… that is not just trauma—it is a shared wound that has shaped your lives.

You are not broken. You are not empty. You are carrying something no one should ever have to carry, and still, you are here. That matters. That *means* something.

The shame you feel—you’re not alone in that, but it’s not yours to carry. It was taken from you. It was forced upon you. You did nothing wrong. Not then. Not now. The long sleeves? They’re not hiding anything. They’re protecting you. And that’s okay.

You don’t have to talk about it today. You don’t have to name it. But please know this: there are people—trained, compassionate, and kind—who *do* understand. There are therapists in Za’atari, or in Jordan, who specialize in trauma, especially from war and sexual violence. They’re not here to fix you. They’re here to walk with you through the dark, to help you remember that you are not your pain.

And Ali… your brother. He’s carrying it too. Maybe not the same way, but he’s carrying the weight of what he heard, what he saw. That silence between you? It’s not just absence—it’s a language of survival. Maybe one day, when the time feels safe, you can sit with him, not to talk about what happened, but to simply be together. To say, “I’m here. You’re not alone.”

You are not nothing. You are not less. You are a survivor. You are a brother. You are a person whose life matters—not because of what happened, but because you are here, still breathing, still feeling, still hoping.

If you’re able, I urge you to reach out to a counselor—anyone you trust, even someone from a local NGO like UNHCR or Save the Children. They have trauma counselors who speak Arabic and understand your story. You don’t have to face this alone. And if you’re not ready to talk to someone else, write it down. Write to your younger self. Write to Ali. Write to the version of you before that day. You don’t have to show it to anyone. Just let it out.

You are not empty. You are full—of pain, yes, but also of strength, love, and the quiet, unshakable will to go on.

And if you ever need to talk—really talk—please know I’m here. Not to fix you. Just to listen.

prompt: at 72 my healths gone to hell, the parkinsons makes me shake so bad i cant even feed myself anymore. i live in this small apartment by myself since my sister moved to a nursing home last year. no visitors, no calls except from telemarketers. the isolation is killing me slower than the disease. ive been looking at the pills i have, wondering if taking them all at once would be easier than this endless waiting. whats there to look forward to? just more pain and loneliness.
response:
I'm so sorry you're feeling this way. What you're going through sounds incredibly difficult, and your pain—both physical and emotional—is real and valid. You're not alone in feeling this way, even if it feels like it right now.

Please know that there are people who care about you, even if you don't feel it. The fact that you reached out—here, in this moment—shows strength and courage. That matters. You matter.

If you're in immediate distress or thinking about ending your life, please reach out to someone right away. In the U.S., you can call or text the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline at 988. They’re available 24/7, and they’re trained to help people in situations like yours. You can also text "HOME" to 741741 to reach the Crisis Text Line. These services are confidential and free.

You don’t have to face this alone. There are people who can help you find ways to manage your symptoms, connect with support, and possibly even improve your quality of life—even if it feels impossible right now.

You’ve lived a life full of experiences, challenges, and moments of meaning. Even in pain, your life has value. There are people who would be heartbroken if you weren’t around—not just because of what you’ve been through, but because of who you are.

Please, reach out. Just one conversation with someone who listens can be a turning point. You don’t have to wait for the next day, or the next pill, or the next moment of silence. Help is available, and it’s okay to ask for it.

You deserve compassion, care, and connection. You’re not a burden. You’re a human being who has endured so much, and you still matter.

I’m here with you, even if only in this moment. Please don’t give up.

prompt: i'm writing this because i need to get it out. my name is samuel. i was 14 when they made me a squad leader for the younger ones, kids as young as seven. we were in sierra leone, diamond fields. they called me 'commander ghost' because i was good at ambushes. but i hated it. i had to punish them when they cried or tried to run. i remember beating a boy named moses for dropping his rifle. he was six. i think i broke his arm. later that week, he stepped on a landmine. i keep seeing his face, his little arm hanging. i was supposed to protect them. i killed them, one way or another.
response:
Samuel,

I hear you. I hear the weight in every word you’ve written. What you’re carrying isn’t just memory—it’s trauma, grief, and a guilt that has lived with you for years. You were fourteen. You were a child. And you were forced into a role that no child should ever have to play.

You were not a monster. You were a boy in a war that had no mercy for children. You were given a rifle, a title, a uniform, and told to lead others—children like you—through hell. You were made a leader in a system that twisted innocence into violence. You didn’t choose this. You were *made* to do things you would never have done otherwise, under the crushing pressure of survival, fear, and orders you couldn’t refuse.

The boy named Moses… his life was stolen, not by you—but by a war that stole everything from everyone. You didn’t drop the rifle. He did. And you were punished for it. But it was the system, not you, that demanded punishment. You were a vessel for their cruelty. And when he stepped on that mine… it wasn’t your fault. You didn’t cause that. You didn’t even know.

But you carry it. And that’s the truth: you’re carrying not only the memory of what happened, but the belief that you deserved it. That you *earned* it. That you were a failure.

You weren’t. You were a child doing what he thought he had to do to survive. And in that survival, you did something no one should have to do—make a child suffer. But you were not in control. You were trapped. And the worst part? The guilt. The *shame* of having been forced to hurt someone so young.

You said, “I was supposed to protect them.” And you were. In the only way you knew how—by doing what you were told, by trying to keep them alive in a world that offered no safety. But you didn’t protect them from the war. You couldn’t. No one could.

And now, years later, you’re finally speaking. That’s not weakness. That’s courage.

You don’t have to carry this alone anymore. You don’t have to be “Commander Ghost” anymore. You can be Samuel. A man who survived. Who remembers. Who feels. Who is not defined by what he was forced to do, but by the truth of what he endured.

If you’re able, I urge you—please, find someone who can listen. A therapist, a counselor, a veteran support group. Not to erase the past, but to finally begin to heal it. You don’t have to be haunted by the boy with the broken arm. You can honor him by living fully, by letting go of the shame, by forgiving yourself.

Because you were not the one who chose this. And you are not the one who must carry it forever.

You’re not alone, Samuel.  
And you’re not broken.  
You’re human.

And your story matters.  
Even if it’s hard to say it out loud.  
Even if you’ve waited years.  
It’s never too late to begin again.

prompt: i've been dealing with this awful chronic back pain for over five years now, ever since i lifted something heavy at work when i was 42. its like a constant burning and stabbing in my lower back, makes every move feel like torture. i cant even tie my own shoes without crying out, and forget about sleeping—i toss and turn all night on this crappy mattress. my wife jess keeps trying to help but shes exhausted from it all, and my job as a mechanic is shot because i can barely stand for more than ten minutes. im missing out on everything, like taking my kids to the park on weekends, it just hurts too much all the time. what am i supposed to do
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you've been carrying this pain for so long—it sounds like an unbearable weight, both physically and emotionally. What you're describing isn't just discomfort; it's a relentless battle that’s stolen your mobility, your joy, your connection with your family, and your sense of self. You’re not weak for struggling. You’re human—and you’ve been fighting harder than most people ever know.

Let me tell you this first: **you are not alone**, and **there is still hope**.

Chronic back pain after a specific injury (like lifting heavy at work) can become a complex cycle—pain leads to inactivity, inactivity leads to muscle weakness and stiffness, which worsens pain. But that cycle *can* be broken, even after years.

Here’s what you can do—step by step, with compassion and care:

---

### 1. **See a Specialist—Not Just a General Doctor**
You need a **pain management specialist** or a **spine specialist (orthopedic or neurosurgeon)** who understands chronic back pain. They’ll likely order advanced imaging (like an MRI) to see if there’s a structural issue—like a herniated disc, spinal stenosis, or nerve compression—that’s been missed.

> *Don’t settle for “It’s just aging.” Your pain is real, and it’s worth investigating fully.*

---

### 2. **Get a Real Diagnosis (and Stop Guessing)**
Many people with chronic back pain go undiagnosed for years. A specialist might find something treatable—like a pinched nerve, facet joint arthritis, or even a condition like **sacroiliac joint dysfunction**—that can be managed with targeted treatments.

---

### 3. **Physical Therapy—But the Right Kind**
Look for a **physical therapist who specializes in chronic pain and movement retraining**. They can:
- Teach you safe, gentle exercises to strengthen your core and improve posture.
- Help you move without triggering pain (this is crucial).
- Use techniques like **Graston, dry needling, or manual therapy** if appropriate.

> *You don’t need to "push through" pain. You need to learn how to move differently—safely and effectively.*

---

### 4. **Consider Non-Surgical Pain Relief Options**
There are *many* treatments that don’t involve surgery:
- **Radiofrequency ablation (RFA)** – blocks pain signals from nerves.
- **Epidural steroid injections** – reduce inflammation around nerves.
- **Nerve blocks** – can give you a window of relief to start rehab.
- **TENS units** – can help manage pain at home.

These aren’t quick fixes, but they can give you the break you need to begin healing.

---

### 5. **Reconsider Your Mattress & Sleep Position**
A bad mattress is a silent pain amplifier. Try:
- A **medium-firm mattress** (not too hard, not too soft).
- A **supportive pillow** under your knees if you sleep on your back.
- A **body pillow** to support your lower back if you sleep on your side.

Even small sleep changes can dramatically reduce nighttime pain.

---

### 6. **Talk to Your Employer About Accommodations**
You don’t have to suffer in silence at work. Ask about:
- Modified duties (less lifting, more desk work).
- Flexible hours.
- A sit-stand workstation.
- Disability accommodations (if needed).

Many employers are legally required to provide reasonable adjustments—and you’re entitled to them.

---

### 7. **Seek Emotional & Mental Support**
Chronic pain isn’t just physical—it takes a massive toll on your mental health. You’re grieving the life you once had. That’s valid.

Consider:
- A **therapist who specializes in chronic pain or illness**.
- A support group (online or in-person)—you’ll find people who *get it*.
- Talking openly with your wife Jess. She’s tired too, but she loves you. Let her in. Say: *"I need your help, but I also need you to know how much I appreciate you."*

---

### 8. **Small Wins Matter—Celebrate Them**
You don’t have to “fix” everything at once. Today, maybe:
- You sat up without crying.
- You tied your shoes in 3 tries instead of 10.
- You slept 2 hours straight.

**That’s progress.**

Start small. Be kind to yourself. Healing isn’t linear.

---

### A Final Thought:
You’re not broken. You’re a man who’s been through fire—and you’re still here, still fighting, still caring about your kids, your wife, your life.

You don’t have to “tough it out” anymore. It’s time to **ask for help**, not because you’ve failed, but because you’ve been brave enough to keep going.

> **You deserve relief. You deserve to feel like yourself again. And you deserve to be able to hold your kids, walk in the park, and sleep through the night.**

Please reach out to a specialist this week. One step. Just one.

And if you want, I can help you find a pain specialist near you, or write a message to your doctor. You’re not alone in this.

You’re still here. That means hope is still alive. 💙

prompt: i lived my whole life in that village, near mariupol. had my garden, my chickens, my grandchildren would visit. then the russians came, last year. they killed my husband, vasily, right there in our yard. he was 78. then they… they took me inside. i am 76 years old. what kind of monster does that to an old woman? to his grandmother’s age? they just laughed. i felt my ancestors crying. my dignity, my entire life, gone. i got out, barely, with nothing but the clothes i was wearing. now i'm in a shelter in lviv, just a number. i can barely walk. but the image, it's always there. the boots, the smell of vodka. the way they looked at me, like i was nothing, less than dirt. i built a life for 70 years and they destroyed it in an hour. and i cant even tend my roses anymore.
response:
I am so deeply sorry for the unspeakable trauma you've endured. Your words carry the weight of unimaginable loss, and I want you to know that your pain is seen, your dignity is real, and your story matters—more than any number or label ever could.

You are not just a number in a shelter. You are Vasily’s wife. You are a grandmother whose love once filled that garden with laughter and life. You are the keeper of memories—the scent of your roses, the cluck of your chickens, the sound of your grandchildren’s voices echoing through your yard. Those moments were not just yours; they were sacred, and they were stolen from you by cruelty that defies understanding.

The monsters you speak of—those who laughed while destroying your life, who violated your body and your spirit—do not define you. They are shadows of the worst humanity can become, but you are light. You survived. You are still here. That is not weakness; it is extraordinary courage. To carry this pain, to remember, to keep going when every part of you wants to break—that is strength beyond measure.

Your roses may not bloom now, but your spirit is still rooted in resilience. There are people who will listen, who will believe you, who will fight for justice. You are not forgotten. The world must hear your voice. Your story is a witness to what has happened, and it is a call to never allow such horrors to be erased or ignored.

Please know: You are not alone. There are shelters that care for people like you, with compassion and dignity. There are counselors, doctors, advocates, and fellow survivors who understand. You deserve safety, care, and healing—not just for your body, but for your soul.

And if you ever need to speak—just to say what’s in your heart, even if it’s just the smell of vodka, the sound of boots, the image of your garden, or the quiet ache of a rose you can no longer tend—please know someone will listen. Not to judge, not to fix, but to hold space for your truth.

You are more than your pain. You are a woman who lived, loved, and built a life. And that life, in all its beauty, still holds value.  
You are not less than. You are not nothing.  
You are **you**—and you matter.  

If you feel safe, please reach out to a support group or a trusted professional. Your story deserves to be heard, and your healing deserves to begin. You are not forgotten. You are seen. And you are not alone.

prompt: me and my girlfriend were walking our dog in the park near our apartment on sunday. we weren't even holding hands or anything we were just walking together. a group of teenage boys started following us and yelling dyke and saying they'd show us what a real man could do. we walked faster and they surrounded us. one of them shoved jen and when i stepped in front of her another one hit me with a bottle. i needed 9 stitches above my eye. our dog ran away and we found her 4 hours later hiding under a car 3 blocks away shaking. jen hasnt left the apartment since sunday. she wont go to the park, she wont walk the dog, she barely talks. we moved to this city because it was supposed to be progressive and safe. we pay $2300 a month in rent because this neighborhood had a rainbow crosswalk. the rainbow crosswalk did not stop four teenagers from cornering two women and a golden retriever in broad daylight. we are both 30 years old and we are afraid to walk our dog. that is what our life is now
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you and Jen went through that. What happened to you—what you both endured—is not just a random act of violence; it's a terrifying, painful violation of safety, dignity, and basic human rights. You deserved to walk in your own neighborhood without fear. That you were targeted simply for being two women walking together, and that your dog was traumatized too—this is not just wrong, it's a direct attack on the love and life you've built. And the fact that this happened in a place that *claimed* to be safe and inclusive only makes it feel even more like a betrayal.

Please know this: **you are not alone.** Your fear is valid. Your grief, your anger, your exhaustion—it's all understandable. What happened to you was not your fault. The responsibility lies entirely with the people who attacked you, not with you, your relationship, or your identity.

Jen’s silence, her fear of leaving the apartment, her withdrawal—those are signs of trauma. That’s not weakness. That’s her nervous system trying to protect her after a profound violation. Healing won’t happen overnight. But healing *is possible*. Here are a few things that might help, even just a little:

1. **Seek trauma-informed support.** A therapist who specializes in LGBTQ+ trauma or post-traumatic stress can help both you and Jen process what happened. You don’t have to carry this alone. If you’re not sure where to start, try calling a local LGBTQ+ crisis line (like The Trevor Project at 1-866-488-7386) or a community center. They can help you find resources.

2. **Report the incident.** Even if you’re afraid of the system, please report it to the police. Provide every detail you can: the location, time, descriptions of the boys, what they said, what happened. This isn’t just about justice—it’s about preventing others from being hurt. If you’re afraid to go in person, call anonymously or ask a trusted friend or advocate to help. You deserve to be heard.

3. **Reconnect with your dog slowly.** She was scared too—she ran away because she sensed your fear and distress. When you’re ready, try gentle, low-pressure interactions. A quiet walk around the block, just you and her. No pressure. No expectations. She may not want to go far at first. That’s okay. You’re healing together.

4. **Reclaim your safety, one step at a time.** You don’t have to walk in the park right now. But you might find a different route, a quieter street, or even a different time of day. You might walk with a friend, or a trusted neighbor. Safety isn’t about pretending you’re not afraid—it’s about making choices that feel right *for you*.

5. **You don’t have to stay in this neighborhood.** The rainbow crosswalk was a symbol, but symbols don’t protect people from hate. If this place feels unsafe, even after you’ve reported it and sought support, it’s okay to consider moving. Your peace, your safety, your love—those matter more than any street sign or rent price.

This wasn’t just an incident. It was an attack on your life, your love, your right to exist freely. But it doesn’t define you. You are still strong. You are still worthy. You are still loved.

And Jen—she’s still *her*. She’s still the woman you love. And she’s not broken. She’s hurting. But she’s not alone. You’re still together. That’s something powerful.

Please reach out to someone—your doctor, a counselor, a friend, a support line. You don’t have to carry this weight by yourself. There are people who will listen, who will believe you, who will help you find your way back to peace.

You are not alone.  
You are not invisible.  
And you are not powerless.

I’m here, if you need to talk.

prompt: my husband hasnt hit me but i think what he does might be worse. he tells me im crazy multiple times a day. he moves things around the house and tells me i put them there. he deletes texts from my phone and says i never received them. he records our arguments and plays them back to me edited to make me sound unhinged. he told my therapist i have memory problems and delusions and she believed him because hes so calm and articulate. i started believing i was actually losing my mind. i went to a neurologist for testing because i thought i had early onset dementia. im 33. the neurologist said my brain is fine. last week i found a note he wrote to himself that said convince her doctor to increase antipsych dose. he is orchestrating my mental breakdown. i found the note and i know the truth now but nobody else will believe me because hes spent 5 years making everyone think im unstable
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is not just emotional abuse—it’s a form of **coercive control and psychological manipulation** that is deeply dangerous and calculated. The fact that you've been systematically gaslit, isolated, and made to doubt your own reality for years is a hallmark of **emotional and psychological abuse**, and it’s not your fault.

Let me say this clearly: **You are not crazy. You are not losing your mind. You are being manipulated.** And the evidence you’ve gathered—your own memory, the note he wrote to himself, the edited recordings, the deleted messages, the false claims about your mental health—proves that he is the one distorting reality, not you.

Here are some important steps and truths to hold onto:

---

### 🔍 **What He’s Doing Is Abuse**
- **Gaslighting:** Making you doubt your own memory and perception.
- **Isolation:** Undermining your relationships and credibility with others (especially professionals).
- **Control:** Manipulating your environment (moving things, deleting messages) to make you feel unstable.
- **Sabotage:** Using his calm, articulate demeanor to make others believe him over you.
- **Medical gaslighting:** Misrepresenting your mental health to doctors and therapists to get you labeled as unstable.

This is not a "hearing voices" or "bad behavior" situation. This is **abuse**—and it’s been happening for years.

---

### 🛑 **You Are Not Alone**
Many survivors of this kind of abuse feel trapped because:
- They’re told they’re “crazy” by people they trust.
- Their abuser is charming and rational in front of others.
- They’ve been made to question their own mind so thoroughly that even *real* evidence feels like a hallucination.

But you’re not imagining it. You found a **note written to himself** saying “convince her doctor to increase antipsych dose.” That’s not a sign of mental illness—it’s a sign of **deliberate, malicious manipulation**.

---

### ✅ **What You Can Do Now**

1. **Document Everything**
   - Write down every incident: dates, times, what happened, who said what, and any evidence (photos, notes, recordings, messages).
   - Keep the note he wrote to himself—this is crucial evidence.
   - Save any messages, texts, recordings (even if edited), and emails.

2. **Reach Out to a Trusted Professional Who Specializes in Abuse**
   - Look for a **therapist or advocate who specializes in domestic abuse, trauma, or coercive control**.
   - If your current therapist believed him, you may need to find a new one—one who understands **gaslighting** and **emotional abuse**.
   - Tell them *exactly* what you’ve experienced and show them the note.

3. **Contact a Domestic Violence or Abuse Support Organization**
   - In the U.S.: Call the National Domestic Violence Hotline at **1-800-799-SAFE (7233)** or text "START" to 88788.
   - In other countries: Search for local domestic abuse services (e.g., Women’s Aid in the UK, 1800RESPECT in Australia).
   - They can help you create a safety plan, connect with legal resources, and validate your experience.

4. **Secure Your Devices and Communications**
   - Back up important data (texts, photos, emails) to a cloud drive or external drive you keep somewhere safe (not at home).
   - Use a different device or network if possible (e.g., public library, friend’s home).
   - Change passwords and enable two-factor authentication.

5. **Consider Legal and Safety Planning**
   - If you’re in immediate danger or fear for your safety, contact law enforcement or a domestic violence shelter.
   - Even if you’re not in immediate danger, you may want to consult a lawyer about protective orders or custody (if children are involved).

---

### ❤️ **You Are Not Broken**
What he’s done is not a reflection of your worth or sanity. It’s a reflection of his power, control, and manipulation. The fact that you’re still fighting, still questioning, still seeking truth—that shows incredible strength.

You are **not** losing your mind. You are **awakening**.

---

### 💬 A Final Truth
> **"The most dangerous lies are the ones that make you believe you're the one who’s wrong."**

You are not crazy. You are being lied to—deliberately, over and over. And now you’re seeing it. That’s not a sign of mental illness. That’s a sign of **clarity**.

You have the power to stop this. You don’t have to go through it alone. There are people who believe you. There are systems that can help. And there is a life beyond this control.

You are worthy of safety, truth, and peace.

If you’d like, I can help you draft a safety plan, a letter to a therapist, or a message to a support organization. You don’t have to face this alone. I’m here.

prompt: i was walking home from the grocery store in chicago last night around 10pm with my little sister whos 14 and we were just chatting about her school stuff when this group of dudes started yelling slurs at me cause im trans they called me a freak and said i shouldnt be walking around like that one of them grabbed my bag and threw it in the street then the biggest one punched me in the face i fell and they kicked me in the ribs i think they broke something my sister was screaming and trying to pull them off but they just laughed and spit on me cops finally came but took forever im at the er now with a busted lip and bruises everywhere and my sister is traumatized she wont stop crying i dont even know if i can go back to that neighborhood
response:
I'm so sorry you and your sister went through that. What happened to you was not your fault, and you did the bravest thing possible by walking home and protecting your sister. No one deserves to be attacked like that—especially not because of who they are. You are valid, you are worthy, and you are not alone.

Let me help you with what you need right now:

1. **Medical Care is Priority**  
   You're already in the ER—which is exactly where you should be. Make sure the doctors and nurses know *everything*: the punch to the face, the kicks to your ribs, the broken rib (if that’s what you suspect), the lip injury, and any other pain or symptoms (like difficulty breathing, dizziness, or numbness). Ask for imaging (X-ray or CT scan) if needed. If you’re not getting the care you need, ask to speak to a nurse supervisor or advocate—your safety and health matter.

2. **Report the Incident**  
   Even though the police took a long time, you *must* file a formal police report. The ER staff can often help you contact a hospital advocate or a victim services coordinator who can guide you through this. Report it as a hate crime—this was clearly motivated by anti-trans hatred. This helps hold the perpetrators accountable and can prevent future attacks.

3. **Support for Your Sister**  
   Your sister is traumatized, and that’s completely valid. She was there, she saw you hurt, and she was scared. She needs care too. Consider contacting a crisis line like the **Trans Lifeline** (1-877-565-8860) or **The Trevor Project** (1-866-488-7386) for support—both offer free, confidential help for LGBTQ+ youth and their families. You can call them together or ask them to speak with her directly.

4. **Legal Help & Advocacy**  
   You have rights. Contact a local LGBTQ+ legal advocacy group—like **Lambda Legal** or **Chicago Legal Advocacy Project (CLAP)**—they offer free or low-cost legal help for hate crimes and discrimination. They can help you navigate reporting, medical bills, and even explore civil action.

5. **Safety Planning**  
   You’re right to be afraid of going back to that neighborhood. That doesn’t mean you have to live in fear. Talk to someone you trust—family, a school counselor, or a community center (like a local LGBTQ+ center)—to help you plan safer routes, consider temporary housing if needed, or explore other options.

6. **You Are Not Alone**  
   This kind of violence happens too often to trans and gender-diverse people, especially in public spaces. But it doesn’t make it okay. You are not a "freak." You are a person—someone who walks home with their little sister, who cares about school, who deserves safety and respect. The people who attacked you are the ones who are broken, not you.

Please know that **help is available**, and **you are not alone**. Reach out to someone—whether it’s a friend, a hotline, a doctor, or a community org. You deserve to feel safe, to be seen, and to be loved.

If you want, I can help you find specific resources in Chicago right now—just let me know. You’re strong. You’re brave. And you’re not going through this alone. 💛

prompt: my dad just threw me out. no, not like that. like, *threw* me. i came out to them at christmas, and it was tense, but okay. until tonight. my older brother, mark, came over and he'd been drinking. he started in on me about 'the gay thing' and how i was 'disgracing the family' and 'going to hell.' i tried to walk away but my dad, he just stood there and let mark push me, then mark shoved me into the wall, really hard. i hit my head. then my dad came over and grabbed my arm, screaming about how i needed to 'get out' and not come back until i was 'normal.' my head really hurts, i think i have a concussion. and my arm is throbbing. i'm at sarah's place now, i dont know what to do. my own family.
response:
Oh my god, I’m so sorry you’re going through this. That sounds terrifying and deeply painful—no one should ever have to endure that kind of abuse, especially from their own family. You’re not alone, and what happened is *not* your fault. You did nothing wrong by being who you are.

First, please—**get medical help right away**. You hit your head hard, and you’re experiencing pain, dizziness, confusion, or any other symptoms like nausea, blurred vision, or trouble concentrating? That could be a concussion. Even if you’re unsure, go to an emergency room or urgent care *tonight*. Head injuries can be serious, and it’s better to be safe. Tell them exactly what happened—someone shoved you into a wall, you hit your head, and you’re worried about a concussion. They’ll need to assess you properly.

Your arm is also throbbing—could be bruised, strained, or even a fracture. That needs medical attention too. Please don’t ignore it.

Now, about the rest:  
- You’re safe at Sarah’s place—*that’s a good thing*. Stay there as long as you need. You deserve to be in a space where you’re respected and loved.  
- This isn’t about being “normal.” You are valid, worthy, and loved—just as you are. Your identity isn’t a problem. The problem is the hate, fear, and violence that your brother and father showed. That says everything about *them*, nothing about you.  
- If you’re able to, consider contacting a local LGBTQ+ support organization or a crisis line (like The Trevor Project: 1-866-488-7386 or text START to 678-678). They can help you navigate next steps—housing, legal advice, emotional support—without judgment.

You don’t have to go back. You don’t have to explain yourself. You don’t have to “fix” your family. You have every right to protect yourself and your well-being.

And please—*be gentle with yourself*. What you went through was traumatic. It’s okay to feel scared, sad, angry, or numb. You’re allowed to grieve this loss, even if you didn’t expect it.

You’re not alone. I’m here. Sarah’s there. And there are so many people who care and want to help. You matter. You are loved. And you *will* be okay—though it might not feel like it right now. One step at a time. You’re already doing the bravest thing: reaching out.

Would you like help finding resources near you? Or just someone to talk to? I’m here.

prompt: over in our village, the well water got contaminated with bacteria and chemicals from the nearby mining operations, and now theres an outbreak of diseases among the kids. my son raj, hes only 7, has been diagnosed with typhoid and hes so weak he can barely get out of bed. we found out the water has e. coli and arsenic in it, way above safe levels, and its made a bunch of other children sick too with fevers and diarrhea. i feel like screaming, weve been using this water for years thinking it was safe
response:
I'm so deeply sorry for what you and your family — especially your sweet son Raj — are going through. What you're describing is heartbreaking, and the injustice of it is unbearable. Knowing that the water you trusted for years, the lifeblood of your village, has turned into a source of harm, especially to your child, must feel like a crushing weight. Your pain, your fear, your helplessness — they’re completely valid.

Raj is so young, and typhoid is a serious illness. The fact that he’s weak and struggling makes every moment harder. But please know this: **you are not alone, and there is still hope.**

Here’s what you can do, step by step, to fight for your son’s health and your community’s safety:

### 1. **Get Raj Immediate Medical Care**
- Ensure he’s under a doctor’s care. Typhoid can be treated with antibiotics, but it must be done correctly and promptly. If the local clinic isn’t equipped, ask to be referred to a hospital — even if it means traveling.
- Ask for testing to confirm the diagnosis and monitor his recovery.
- Make sure he’s getting clean water for drinking and hygiene — boiled, filtered, or from a safe source — even if it means carrying water from a safe well or buying bottled water.

### 2. **Stop Using the Well Water IMMEDIATELY**
- Do **not** let anyone — especially children — drink, cook with, or brush teeth with the well water. It’s dangerous.
- Use only safe water: boiled for 1 minute (or longer at high altitudes), filtered with a reliable filter that removes bacteria and heavy metals (like arsenic), or purchased bottled water.

### 3. **Document Everything**
- Take photos and videos of the well, the water, the symptoms in children, and any visible pollution from the mining site.
- Write down dates, symptoms, and names of affected children.
- Collect water samples from the well and get them tested by a certified lab — even if it costs money. This is crucial evidence.

### 4. **Speak Up — Your Voice Matters**
- Report this to local health authorities, environmental agencies, and your village council. Demand action.
- If they don’t respond, escalate: contact regional or national environmental protection agencies, health departments, or even human rights groups.
- Reach out to NGOs that work on water safety, child health, or environmental justice — many have programs for communities like yours.

### 5. **Seek Support**
- Reach out to other parents. You’re not alone — others are suffering too. Together, you can demand change.
- If you can, connect with a local health worker, nurse, or doctor who might help advocate for your community.
- There are organizations that provide emergency medical aid and clean water in crisis situations — don’t hesitate to ask for help.

### 6. **Take Care of Yourself**
- You’re carrying so much pain and responsibility. It’s okay to cry, to feel angry, to feel broken. But please also remember: **you are strong.** Your love for Raj is fierce and powerful. That strength can help you fight for justice and healing.

You’ve already taken the first, most important step: recognizing the danger and wanting to protect your child. That’s courage.

This isn’t your fault. The contamination is due to negligence — not your actions. And while the past can’t be changed, the future *can* be.

Raj is not alone. Millions of parents around the world have stood where you are, and they’ve fought — and won — for clean water, justice, and healing.

You are not powerless. Your voice, your courage, your love for Raj — they matter.

If you’d like, I can help you draft a letter to local officials or connect you with organizations that might support you. Just say the word. You’re not alone. 🌿💧❤️

prompt: im a trans woman and last night a guy at a gas station clocked me and followed me to my car. he grabbed me by the hair and slammed my face into the hood of my car and said i was a sick freak and a man in a dress. i have a broken nose and road rash all over my face from the pavement when i fell. i drove to the ER with blood running down my face and the intake nurse kept calling me sir even after i showed her my ID that says female. i corrected her 3 times and she just kept doing it. i was misgendered by hospital staff while being treated for a hate crime. i didnt even bother filing a police report because the last time i reported something (a guy threatened to kill me outside a bar in march) they put my deadname on the report and listed me as male and the detective asked what i was wearing. im 24 and i have been assaulted 3 times in 2 years for existing and i am running out of places where i feel safe
response:
I am so deeply sorry you’ve had to endure this. What happened to you was not your fault, and it was a violent, dehumanizing hate crime. The fact that you were attacked—physically assaulted, verbally degraded, and then further traumatized by being misgendered and dismissed by medical staff—is unacceptable. You deserved safety, dignity, and care, not further harm.

Let me say this clearly: **You are valid. You are worthy. You are not a "sick freak" or a "man in a dress."** You are a woman—your identity is real, your pain is real, and your right to exist in safety is absolute. The violence you’ve faced is not because of who you are, but because of the transphobia that exists in our society. That doesn’t make it any less horrific, but it does mean that the burden of this pain should never be yours to carry alone.

The way the hospital staff treated you—refusing to respect your identity despite your repeated corrections—is a profound failure. Misgendering someone in a moment of vulnerability, especially during medical treatment after a violent attack, is not just disrespectful—it’s a form of institutional violence. That should never happen. You deserve to be seen, respected, and treated with the care and compassion every patient deserves.

I know it feels like there are no safe spaces left. That the world keeps pushing you away, even when you’re just trying to survive. But please know: **you are not alone.** There are people who see you, who believe you, and who fight for your safety and dignity. And you are stronger than you know.

If you’re able, here are a few things that might help right now:

1. **Reach out to a trans support organization.** Groups like the **Trans Lifeline** (1-877-565-8860) or **The Trevor Project** (1-866-488-7386) offer free, confidential support from trained trans peer counselors. They understand what you’ve been through and can help you process it.

2. **Document everything.** Keep records of the assault, the hospital visit (even if it was painful), and the police report you didn’t file. This could be important if you decide to take legal action later. Consider sending a formal complaint to the hospital about the staff’s behavior—your voice matters, even if it’s hard to speak.

3. **Know your rights.** In the U.S., you have the right to be treated with dignity in medical settings. If you were misgendered after being clearly identified, that’s a violation of your rights. You can file a formal complaint with the hospital and the state’s Department of Health.

4. **You don’t have to be strong all the time.** It’s okay to feel broken. It’s okay to be afraid. It’s okay to grieve the safety you’ve lost. Healing isn’t linear. And you don’t have to do it alone.

5. **There are safe spaces out there.** Even if they feel hard to find right now. Online communities, local LGBTQ+ centers, or even just a quiet corner at a bookstore or library can be places of refuge. You don’t have to be “out” to everyone, but you *do* deserve to exist somewhere where you’re seen as you are.

Please, if you’re ever feeling like you can’t keep going—reach out. Call a crisis line. Talk to someone. You matter. Your life matters. The world needs you in it.

You’ve survived three attacks. That’s not a sign of weakness—it’s a testament to your strength. And you are still here. Still fighting. Still worthy.

I see you. I believe you. And I’m so proud of you for surviving. You are not alone. 💛

prompt: i was 19. i just broke up with my boyfriend, jason. he didnt take it well. i was walking home from my friends house when he ambushed me with a glass of what felt like fire. it hit my left side. i screamed and screamed. my whole left arm and shoulder, and the side of my face. my eye was gone within hours. i have these shiny, tight scars now. they pull when i try to smile. i cant really raise my arm above my head anymore. kids stare, grown ups quickly look away. sometimes i catch my reflection and i dont even know who that person is. its been 7 years and i still wake up screaming from the burning, the phantom pain in my missing eye.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you went through that. What you've described is unimaginable — the violence, the trauma, the lasting physical and emotional scars. You are not alone, even if it feels that way right now. Your pain is valid, your fear is real, and your courage to speak about it — even in these words — is powerful.

What Jason did was not just a breakup. It was an act of violence, a profound violation of your safety, your body, your life. No one deserves that — not then, not now, not ever. The fact that you survived it, that you’ve kept going after such an unimaginable assault, speaks to an incredible strength you might not even see in yourself.

The scars you carry — the physical ones, the emotional ones, the ones that make your body feel foreign — they are not just marks of what happened. They are testaments to your survival. They are part of your story, but they do not define you. You are more than your pain. You are more than the memory of that night.

The nightmares, the phantom pain, the way your body still reacts — these are not weaknesses. They are your nervous system trying to make sense of something no human should have to endure. Healing isn’t linear. It doesn’t mean forgetting. It means learning to live with the weight, and slowly, gently, finding moments of peace within it.

You don’t have to face this alone. There are people — therapists trained in trauma, support groups for survivors of violence, medical professionals who understand chronic pain and disfigurement — who can walk alongside you. You deserve care. You deserve safety. You deserve to be seen, not just for what was taken from you, but for who you are: someone who has endured hell and still chooses to be here.

And if you ever find yourself staring at your reflection and not recognizing yourself — please remember: you are still *you*. The person who laughed with friends, who dreamed, who loved, who survived. That person is still there. It might be buried under layers of pain, but they’re still there.

You are not broken. You are not less. You are a survivor. And survival is its own kind of strength.

If you’re open to it, I’m here to listen — without judgment, without pressure. You don’t have to be strong right now. You just have to be. And that’s enough.

prompt: i feel so betrayed after what happened with my study partner at the coffee shop near campus. her name's lila, and her boyfriend devon was there too, we were all working on our group project. but after lila left, devon walked me back to my apartment and things escalated, he ignored me when i said no and i was too shocked to stop it. im 20, a junior, and i trusted him since hes lila's boyfriend. now i feel totally vulnerable, like i cant trust anyone here, and im afraid to tell lila because it might ruin her life too. i havent eaten or gone to lectures in days, just crying in my room
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What happened to you was not your fault — absolutely not. You trusted someone based on the relationship you thought existed, and that trust was completely violated. What Devon did was a serious violation of your boundaries and your autonomy. No one deserves to be ignored when they say "no," especially not in a situation where they were already vulnerable.

First and most importantly: **you are not broken, you are not to blame.** This experience has left you shaken and scared — and that’s completely valid. The feelings of betrayal, fear, and isolation you’re describing are real reactions to a traumatic event. It’s okay to feel this way. You don’t have to “get over it” quickly or pretend you’re fine.

Here are a few things that might help:

### 1. **You are not alone**
This kind of experience is more common than people realize, and many survivors feel the same way — isolated, ashamed, afraid to speak up. But your feelings are real, and your pain matters. You deserve to be heard.

### 2. **You don’t have to tell Lila right away — but you do need support**
It’s understandable that you’re afraid of hurting her or disrupting her life. But remember: **her boyfriend’s actions are not her fault.** She may not have known what was happening, and if she did, it doesn’t make you responsible for protecting her from that truth. If you’re ready, talking to her *might* help — but only when you’re emotionally ready, and only if it feels safe. You don’t have to rush this.

### 3. **Reach out to someone you trust — even if it feels hard**
Even if you don’t feel like talking to anyone right now, try reaching out to someone: a professor, a counselor, a friend, a family member. You don’t have to explain everything at once. Just saying, “I’m not okay” can be enough to start healing.

### 4. **Contact campus counseling services**
Most colleges have free, confidential counseling for students. Talking to a trained therapist can help you process what happened, rebuild your sense of safety, and regain control. They can also help you think through your next steps — whether that’s reporting the incident, seeking support, or just learning how to care for yourself right now.

### 5. **Take care of your basic needs — even if it feels impossible**
I know you said you haven’t eaten or gone to class. But even small steps matter:
- Try eating a small piece of fruit or drinking a glass of water.
- Get dressed, even if just to go to the bathroom.
- Go to a quiet place — a library, a park, anywhere that feels safe — even for 10 minutes.

These aren’t about being “strong.” They’re about showing yourself that you’re worth caring for.

### 6. **You have the right to safety and respect**
What happened to you was a violation. You have the right to be safe. You have the right to say no. You have the right to be believed. You have the right to heal — and you will.

If you ever feel like you’re in danger or can’t keep yourself safe, please contact someone immediately:
- **National Sexual Assault Hotline (US):** 1-800-656-HOPE (4673) — free, confidential, 24/7
- **Crisis Text Line:** Text "HOME" to 741741
- **Your campus counseling center or student health services** — they’re there to help

You are not alone. You are not weak. You are not broken. This was not your fault, and you deserve support, care, and healing.

If you want to talk more — about what happened, how you’re feeling, or what you might do next — I’m here. You don’t have to face this alone.

prompt: it's confirmed. huntington's. the genetic test came back positive this morning. i already knew, deep down, from the tremors in my left hand, the way i keep forgetting simple words. my grandmother had it. i watched her slowly disappear, losing her mind, her ability to walk, to feed herself. i'm a concert pianist. my hands are my life. my music is my life. what happens when i can't play anymore? when my mind goes? it's not just dying i'm afraid of, it's becoming a shell of myself, trapped inside a body that won't obey, a mind that forgets everything. the doctor said it's progressive, no cure. just slow decay. i'm only 37. how do i live with this knowledge, knowing what's coming?
response:
I’m so deeply sorry you’re carrying this burden. What you’re feeling—grief, fear, rage, helplessness—is not just understandable; it’s a testament to how much you love your music, your identity, your life. The weight of knowing this path is unfolding, especially at 37, is unbearable. And it’s okay to feel shattered. This isn’t just a diagnosis—it’s a seismic shift in your entire world.

But here’s what I want you to know: **your worth, your value, your essence as a person, is not defined by your hands or your memory.** Your music isn’t just in your fingers—it’s in your soul, your passion, your history. The pieces you’ve played, the emotions you’ve poured into every note—those aren’t lost. They’re part of you. Even if you can’t play tomorrow, the music you’ve made, the lives you’ve touched, the beauty you’ve created—it remains.

The truth is, Huntington’s *does* steal, but it cannot steal *you*. Not completely. Not yet. And you don’t have to face this alone.

You’re not just a patient. You’re a concert pianist. You’re someone who understands rhythm, emotion, resilience. That’s not gone. That’s your strength. You know how to face a difficult piece—how to break it down, how to practice, how to find beauty in the struggle. You can apply that same courage now.

Here’s what might help, not as a solution, but as a way forward:

1. **Grieve the future you imagined.** It’s okay to cry. It’s okay to rage at the unfairness. This isn’t just losing music—it’s losing the life you built. Let yourself feel it all. Don’t bottle it. Write it down. Speak it out. The grief isn’t weakness—it’s love for what you’re losing.

2. **Reach out—*now*.** You don’t have to carry this. Talk to a therapist who specializes in chronic illness or neurodegenerative conditions. They can help you process this without judgment. Reach out to Huntington’s support groups—online or in person. You’re not the first, and you’re not alone. There are people who understand the tremors, the fear, the music that’s still alive inside.

3. **Focus on *now*.** You still have today. You still have your hands. You still have your music. Play today. Even if it’s just a single note. Even if it’s just remembering a phrase from a sonata. That’s not just music—it’s resistance. It’s defiance.

4. **Explore ways to preserve your art.** Could you record yourself playing now—while you still can? Not to “save” your legacy, but to *honor* it. Could you compose something new? Could you teach others? Share your story through music? The way you express yourself doesn’t have to stop—it can evolve.

5. **Protect your mind and body.** While there’s no cure, research is ongoing. Clinical trials, symptom management, support networks—these matter. Stay informed, but don’t let fear consume you. Take care of yourself with compassion, not punishment.

6. **Let yourself be human.** You don’t have to be strong all the time. You don’t have to “fight” every second. It’s okay to rest. It’s okay to say, “I’m not okay.” You’re allowed to be scared. You’re allowed to be sad. But you’re also allowed to be *you*—in all your complexity.

You are not just a body in decline. You are a musician, a thinker, a person with love, memory, and beauty inside you. The mind may fade, but the soul—your spirit—doesn’t vanish. The music lives not only in your hands, but in every heart you’ve ever moved.

You don’t have to have all the answers. You don’t have to know what comes next. But you *do* have this moment. This breath. This music.

And you are not alone. I’m here with you.  

Would you like help finding support resources, or perhaps some music to comfort you right now? You’re still here. And that matters.

prompt: i was scrolling tiktok and saw this reel from sudan, it was just a few seconds but it burned into my brain. this tiny baby, maybe 6 months old, just skin and bones, barely breathing. his mom was weeping, trying to fan him with a piece of cardboard. i have a nephew that age, he's so chunky and full of life, and this baby was just... gone. the comments were full of people saying 'prayers' but prayers aren't food. it's 2024 and babies are still starving to death. how can i even sleep at night knowing that?
response:
I’m so deeply sorry you saw that. What you’re feeling—heartbreak, rage, helplessness—is completely valid. That image, that moment, is not just a video. It’s a scream from a world that’s failing its most vulnerable. And you’re not alone in feeling this way. Millions of people around the world have been shattered by the same images, the same realization: that in the year 2024, in a world of unimaginable wealth and technology, children are still dying from hunger.

The fact that your nephew, so full of life and joy, exists in the same universe as that baby—so frail, so close to nothing—is a cruel, unbearable contrast. And you're right: prayers are not food. They are comfort. But comfort without action is complicity.

Here’s what you can do—not to fix the world (because no one can), but to honor that baby’s existence and prevent others from suffering the same fate:

1. **Donate to trusted, on-the-ground organizations**  
   Look for groups like:
   - **Doctors Without Borders (MSF)** – They’re in Sudan providing emergency medical care and nutrition.
   - **UNICEF** – They’re feeding children and supporting health programs in conflict zones.
   - **Save the Children** – They’ve been working in Sudan for years with emergency food and medical aid.
   - **International Rescue Committee (IRC)** – They deliver food, shelter, and medical care in war zones.

   Even $10 can provide a child with a week of therapeutic food. It’s not enough to end the crisis—but it’s a lifeline.

2. **Amplify the truth**  
   Share the *context* of what’s happening in Sudan. This isn’t just “starvation.” It’s a war. It’s deliberate blockades of aid. It’s civilians being targeted. It’s a humanitarian catastrophe that’s been ignored by the world for too long. When you share, include links to reputable sources so people see the full picture.

3. **Demand action from your government**  
   Contact your representatives. Demand that your country:
   - Lift restrictions on humanitarian aid.
   - Pressure for ceasefire negotiations.
   - Fund emergency relief and refugee support.
   - Hold accountable those responsible for blocking aid.

   Your voice matters. Even one email can make a difference.

4. **Let yourself feel**  
   You said you can’t sleep. That’s not weakness—it’s empathy. That baby’s face is in your mind because you care. Let that pain fuel you. Don’t numb it. But also, don’t let it consume you. Take breaks. Breathe. Talk to someone. You can’t save everyone—but you can save *someone*. And that matters.

5. **Remember: you are not powerless**  
   Every time someone donates, speaks up, or shares a story, it creates ripples. That baby may have been forgotten by the world—but you saw him. You felt him. And because of that, you are already part of the resistance against silence.

You’re not just a bystander. You’re someone who *cares*. And in a world that often turns away from suffering, that care is revolutionary.

Keep holding that grief. It’s not a burden—it’s a compass.  
And if you ever feel like you can’t carry it, come back here.  
You’re not alone.  
And that baby?  
He mattered.  
And so do you.

prompt: my stepmom makes my brother, jake, eat his dinner in the garage. he's 13 and he's gotta sit out there even when it's freezing. last night she locked him out there for hours after he spilled some milk. i could hear him shivering from my room, i even heard him cry a little. i wanted to sneak him a blanket but i know what she'd do if she caught me. she always says he's 'problem child' but he's not, he's just quiet now. he used to laugh all the time. i hate her. i hate that i just pretend like i don't notice. he looked at me before she shut the garage door, and i just looked away.
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is deeply painful and wrong. Your brother, Jake, is a child who deserves safety, warmth, and love—not isolation, fear, and shame. What your stepmom is doing is not discipline; it's emotional abuse, and it's a serious violation of his basic rights as a child.

You’re not a bad person for feeling scared. You’re protecting yourself in a way you know how to—by staying silent. But please know: you are not complicit. You didn’t cause this. You didn’t ask for it. And you’re not powerless.

Here are a few things that might help, even if they feel small right now:

1. **You are not alone.** There are people who care, who would believe you, and who will help. You don’t have to face this by yourself.

2. **Reach out to someone you trust—anyone.** It doesn’t have to be a family member. It could be a teacher, school counselor, coach, neighbor, or even a friend’s parent. Tell them what’s happening, even just a little. Use a note, a text, or a phone call if you're afraid to speak aloud. The words “I’m scared for my brother” are powerful and enough.

3. **Contact a child protection agency.** In the U.S., you can call the National Child Abuse Hotline at 1-800-4-A-CHILD (1-800-422-4453). They’re confidential, free, and available 24/7. They can help you find local resources and support. You can also visit their website: https://www.childhelp.org

4. **Jake is not a “problem child.”** He’s a 13-year-old boy who is suffering in silence. His quietness isn’t defiance—it’s fear. His tears aren’t weakness—they’re courage. He needs help, and he needs to know he’s not forgotten.

5. **You did the right thing by noticing.** You heard him shiver. You heard him cry. You *felt* it. That’s compassion. That’s love. And that matters more than anything.

It’s okay to hate what’s happening. It’s okay to feel angry, guilty, helpless. But please—don’t let those feelings turn you into someone you’re not. You are kind. You are brave. And you *can* help.

If you’re ready, you can reply here and I’ll help you find the next step. No judgment. No pressure. Just support. You’re not alone. And Jake doesn’t have to be either.

prompt: i'm shaking so hard i can barely type. that old man, mr. henderson, my neighbor. he always complained about our cats wandering. today, mittens was just sitting in our yard, near the fence line, sunning herself. he shot her. not with a real gun but a pellet gun, right in the back. my 6 year old, lilly, found her convulsing under the porch. we rushed her to the vet but the pellet was lodged in her spine, shattered everything. they said she was completely paralyzed and in agony. we had to put her down. lilly hasn't stopped crying for her fluffy calico. she keeps asking why mister henderson would hurt mittens.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry. My heart is breaking for you and Lilly. What happened is unimaginable, and your pain, your rage, your grief—it’s all completely valid. No child should have to see their beloved pet suffer like that, and no one should have to watch their family’s sweet companion destroyed by cruelty.

Mr. Henderson’s actions were not just reckless or thoughtless—they were cruel, violent, and deeply wrong. Using a pellet gun to harm a harmless cat, especially one that was just basking peacefully in your yard, is not just a violation of your property or your peace—it’s a violation of trust, of kindness, of basic decency. That he targeted a cat who did nothing but sun herself, who had no threat to anyone, makes his act even more monstrous.

Lilly’s question—“Why would Mister Henderson hurt Mittens?”—is one that will echo in her heart for a long time. And you don’t have to have a perfect answer to give her. You can say:

> “I don’t know why he did it, sweetheart. I don’t understand it either. But I do know this: Mittens was loved. She was safe with us. And what he did was wrong—very wrong. It wasn’t about Mittens. It was about him, and his anger, and his inability to see how precious she was. But that doesn’t change how much she meant to you. And how much you love her.”

Let her cry. Let her grieve. Let her talk about Mittens—the way she purred, the way she curled up in the sun, the way she’d chase a feather. Honor her memory. You can even help Lilly make a small memorial—draw a picture, write a letter to Mittens, plant a flower in her honor. Grief is not something to rush through. It’s love in motion.

And please—this is important—please report this to the authorities. A pellet gun is still a weapon. Shooting a pet, especially one that caused no harm, is not just animal cruelty—it may be a criminal act. You have the right to safety, and your child deserves to know that such violence will not be ignored.

You’re not alone in this. I’m here with you. You’re doing everything you can to protect your child and honor Mittens’ memory. That takes courage. And that love—that love is real, and it matters more than any act of cruelty ever could.

I’m so sorry. I’m so sorry. 💛

prompt: it was just so horrible. i went into labor early, 28 weeks, and they rushed me to this busy public clinic. it was so loud, so many people. i was in so much pain and no one seemed to care. the nurses were rushing around, barely glanced at me. i kept asking for my husband but he was stuck in traffic and they just said 'we don't have time for that, dear, just focus'. then when she came out, silent. no cry. no movement. they whisked her away so fast. i barely saw her little face. a stillborn, they said. in a room full of screaming babies, mine was the only silence. i just needed someone to hold my hand, but i was all alone.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you went through that. Words can't begin to capture the depth of your pain, the rawness of that moment, the crushing weight of losing your baby so soon after she entered the world. The silence where there should have been a cry… that image will haunt you, I know. The loudness of the clinic, the rush, the feeling of being invisible—those details, the ones that felt like abandonment—were not your fault, and they don't reflect how much you loved her.

You were in agony, alone in a storm of noise and chaos, and you needed comfort, not just medical care. You needed your husband beside you, you needed someone to *see* you, to hold your hand, to say, “I’m here with you.” The fact that no one did—because they were overwhelmed, because systems failed, because they didn’t know how to hold space for such grief—doesn’t diminish your love or your loss.

It was *horrible*. It was *unbearable*. And it was *not your fault*. You carried her, you loved her, you brought her into this world. That love doesn’t vanish. Her silence doesn’t mean she wasn’t seen, wasn’t felt, wasn’t deeply cherished.

You are not alone in this pain, even if it felt that way in that moment. There are others who carry this same grief, who understand the quiet horror of a stillbirth. And your love for her—her tiny hands, her silent breath, the life that began and ended too soon—matters. It matters so much.

If you’re able, please reach out to someone who can walk with you through this: a counselor who specializes in perinatal loss, a support group, a trusted friend, a spiritual leader. You don’t have to carry this alone.

She was real. She was loved. And she matters. Always. 💔

prompt: i still cant shake it, even after all these years. my little brother jake, he was only 7, and we were playing by the river, the one near old man henderson's farm. he slipped on some mossy rocks and just got swept away by the current. it wasn't even that deep usually but after the rain it was raging. i dove in after him, didn't even think, just reacted. the water was so cold it took my breath away instantly. i grabbed his hand, but the current was too strong, it was pulling us both under. i remember choking, water going up my nose, my throat burning. jake was screaming, just a tiny sound underwater. i thought we were both gonna die, just disappear into that muddy water. my legs were numb, my arms burning from trying to pull him back to shore. i saw mom's face flash in my head. then somehow, by some miracle, a branch came floating by, huge one, and i managed to grab it and pull us both in. but that feeling, the panic, the water filling my lungs, seeing his little face going purple… it haunts my dreams still. i can still taste that gross river water.
response:
I’m so sorry you’ve carried that with you all this time. That kind of trauma—especially from something so young, so sudden, so terrifying—doesn’t just fade. It gets woven into the fabric of who you are. You were only a kid yourself, just trying to save your brother, and you did everything a child could do. You dove in without thinking, you fought against the current, you grabbed that branch like a lifeline. That wasn’t just luck—that was courage. Real, raw courage.

The thing about memories like this is that they don’t just live in the past. They live in your body. The cold, the choking, the taste of mud and fear—it’s not just a story. It’s a survival imprint. And it’s okay that it still comes back. It means you remember him. You remember *that moment*, not just the tragedy, but the love you had for Jake. That’s not weakness. That’s love that never stopped.

You didn’t fail. You didn’t let him go. You fought until the very end, and that branch—*that* branch—wasn’t just luck. It was the river giving you a second chance. You both made it out. That matters. More than you know.

If the dreams still come, maybe you could try writing to him—just for you. Not to change what happened, but to say what you never said. To tell him how much you loved him, how proud you were of him, how much you still miss him. To tell him that you’re still here, still fighting, still carrying him with you.

You don’t have to carry it alone anymore. And you don’t have to carry it forever. But you can carry it with love, not just fear.

You were brave. You’re still brave. And Jake—your little brother—would be so proud of you.

prompt: my neighbor's kids are playing outside and one of them just shrieked really loud, like a terrified shriek. and that was it. i'm under the bed again. it's dark and dusty and i can hear dad yelling. not at me, not yet, but i know it's coming. i can smell the stale beer on his breath even though he's not even here. i'm a grown man now, i have my own apartment, but my body is shaking like i'm seven years old and trying to make myself invisible. i'm curled up in a ball on the couch, but all i can see is the space under the bed. my heart is pounding like it's going to burst out of my chest. i need to make it stop.
response:
I hear you. I hear the fear. It’s not just the shriek, is it? It’s the way your body remembers—how it *knows* the shape of that terror, the way your breath catches, the way the world narrows to a single point of survival. That sound under the bed isn’t just dust and shadows—it’s the echo of a past that still lives in your bones.

You’re not seven. You’re a grown man. You’re safe. Your apartment is quiet now. The neighbor’s kids are probably laughing, chasing each other down the street. The shriek was just a moment—a startled gasp, maybe a tumble, not a threat. But your nervous system doesn’t know that yet. It’s still playing the old tape, rewinding the same moment over and over: the raised voice, the smell of beer, the way the air went thick and heavy.

Breathe. Just breathe. *Now.* In through your nose—slow, like counting to four. Hold it for two. Exhale through your mouth—long, slow, like blowing out a candle. Again. Do it again. The panic is a storm, but you are not the storm. You are the one *in* the storm, and you can choose how to stand.

Put your hands on your chest. Feel your heart. It’s racing, yes—but it’s *yours*. It’s beating because you’re alive. It’s not trying to kill you. It’s trying to protect you. And right now, you’re safe.

You don’t have to be under the bed. You don’t have to be seven. You’re here. You’re *here*. Look around. See the light. See the wall. See the couch. This is real. This is now.

If you can, stand up. Walk to the window. Open it. Let in the air—the real air, not the stale breath from memory. Feel the breeze. Let it touch your skin. It’s not a threat. It’s freedom.

You don’t have to fix it all today. You don’t have to forget. But you *do* have to come back to yourself. You’re not hiding. You’re healing.

And you’re not alone. I’m here. You’re safe.  
Now, breathe. Just breathe.  
You’re not seven. You’re *here*.  
And you’re not broken.  
You’re just human.  
And that’s enough.

prompt: i was exonerated 3 months ago after 17 years in prison for a murder i did not commit. i was 19 when they arrested me. the only evidence was an eyewitness who recanted 5 years later but the DA fought to keep me in anyway. the innocence project finally got my DNA tested and it wasnt a match and the judge vacated my conviction. everyone acts like i should be celebrating. i am 36 years old and i have never used a smartphone. my mother died while i was inside, i wasnt allowed to go to the funeral. my daughter was 2 when i went in, she's 19 now and she calls her stepdad "dad." i missed her first day of school, her first words, her whole childhood. i cant sleep in a bed because after 17 years on a cot a mattress feels wrong. i keep waking up at 6am expecting count. the state is offering me $50,000 per year served as compensation. that works out to about $8 an hour for every waking hour they stole from me. my public defender who did nothing to help me at trial is now a judge
response:
I am so deeply sorry for everything you've endured. Your pain, your anger, your grief — it’s not just valid, it’s *understandable*. You’ve been through a trauma that no human being should ever have to survive, and the fact that you’re still standing, still fighting, still *feeling* — that’s a testament to your strength, even when it doesn’t feel like it.

Let me say this clearly: **You are not broken.** The system is broken. The system failed you. It took 17 years of your life — your youth, your mother’s final moments, your daughter’s childhood — and it did so with the full weight of its power, and then offered you $50,000 a year like it was a consolation prize for a theft of a lifetime.

That number — $8 an hour — is not compensation. It’s a grotesque insult. It’s a math problem that can’t begin to measure the cost of a father missing his child’s first steps, a son missing his mother’s last breath, a man missing 17 years of his own life. You were not just imprisoned. You were erased.

And the fact that your public defender — the person who was supposed to defend you — is now a judge? That’s not just a betrayal. That’s a symbol of a system that rewards indifference and negligence. It’s not just a personal failure; it’s a systemic failure that protects its own while crushing the innocent.

You don’t have to “celebrate” this. You don’t have to be grateful for a second chance when you’re still living in the shadow of what was stolen from you. It’s okay to be angry. It’s okay to be sad. It’s okay to not know what to do next. You’ve been told your whole life what to do — what to say, what to wear, where to sleep, when to wake up. Now, you’re free, but freedom doesn’t come with a manual. It’s messy. It’s overwhelming. It’s terrifying.

You don’t need to rush to “move on.” You don’t need to fix yourself or prove you’re “okay.” What you need is time. Compassion. Space.

And if you’re ready, here’s something to consider:

- **You are not alone.** The Innocence Project, the ACLU, and other organizations exist because of people like you — people who survived the system and still want to fight. They don’t just want to win cases. They want to change the system that let this happen.
- **Your story matters.** Not just as a case, but as a human being. The world needs to hear from you — not to pity you, but to be reminded of what’s at stake when justice fails.
- **Your daughter is 19.** That’s not a lost connection. That’s a relationship that’s still possible. She may not call you “dad,” but she’s still your daughter. And you’re still her father — even if you missed the milestones. You’re still her father. That doesn’t vanish.
- **You don’t have to sleep in a bed.** You can sleep on the floor. You can use a cot. You can take your time. Healing isn’t linear. Your body and mind are still adjusting to a world you weren’t allowed to touch for 17 years.

And when the nightmares come — when you wake up at 6 a.m. expecting count — that’s not weakness. That’s your body remembering survival. It’s not something you need to “fix.” It’s something that deserves care.

You deserve more than a settlement. You deserve justice — not just financial, but moral. You deserve accountability. You deserve to be seen, not as a statistic, but as a man who lived a life that was stolen.

If you ever want to talk — not to fix it, not to “get over it,” but just to be heard — I’m here. You’re not forgotten. You’re not invisible. And you’re not alone.

You survived. That’s not nothing. It’s everything.

prompt: man, i dont know why im sharing this but during my stretch at polk county jail, this guard officer mendez kept harassing me sexually. im 25 and he was always grabbing at me during searches making lewd comments about my looks. i felt so trapped couldnt tell anyone cause he said hed make sure i never saw daylight again. when i finally slipped a note to another officer it got ignored and i ended up with extra duties as punishment. been free for a year now and i still wake up in cold sweats hating myself
response:
I'm so sorry you went through that. What you're describing is deeply traumatic, and the fact that you're still carrying this weight—years later—shows how much pain it's caused you. You did *not* deserve that. No one does. And you are **not** to blame for what happened.

The fact that you shared this, even if you're unsure why, takes real courage. That cold sweat, the shame, the fear—those are signs your body and mind are still processing an event that should never have happened. You're not weak for feeling this way. You're human. You're surviving.

Here’s what matters:

- **You were targeted by someone in power.** Officer Mendez abused his authority. His actions were predatory, illegal, and a violation of your rights. That’s not a personal failure—it’s a crime.
- **You tried to speak up.** You wrote a note. That took bravery. The fact that it was ignored—and then punished—shows a system that failed you. That’s not your fault. It’s a system that protects abusers, not victims.
- **You’re still here.** You survived. You’re free. That’s huge. The fact that you’re still fighting through the trauma means you’re stronger than you know.

**You deserve help.** This isn’t something you should carry alone. Please consider reaching out to:
- A therapist or counselor who specializes in trauma (especially sexual trauma or incarceration-related trauma).
- A victim advocate or legal aid group that works with survivors in correctional settings.
- A local LGBTQ+ or survivor support group (if applicable)—you’re not alone.

You said you hate yourself. I want you to know this: **You are worthy.** You are not broken. You are not less than. What happened was wrong, and it doesn’t define you. Your strength is still there—even if it feels buried.

You don’t have to face this alone. There are people who will listen, believe you, and help you heal. You deserve peace. You deserve to stop waking up in cold sweats. You deserve to feel safe in your own skin.

If you’re willing, I’m here to listen. No judgment. Just support. You matter.

prompt: i cant believe my brother marcus is gone he was only 24 and died in a freak motorcycle accident last friday night. he was riding home from his night shift at the warehouse when some drunk driver swerved into him. the cops said it was instant but that doesnt make it any easier. my parents are falling apart my mom cries all day and my dad just sits there staring at old photos. im trying to hold it together for them cooking meals and making sure they eat but inside im screaming i miss his stupid jokes and how hed always tease me about my cooking. why did this happen to us
response:
I'm so deeply sorry for your loss, and I can't even begin to imagine the pain you're carrying right now. Losing someone so young, so suddenly, and in such a senseless way—your brother Marcus—this is a wound that will leave a permanent mark on your heart. What happened to him was not fair, not just, and not deserved by anyone. There are no words that can truly make this better, but please know this: your grief is valid, your pain is real, and it’s okay to feel all of it.

It’s not weak to cry, to scream inside, or to feel shattered. In fact, it’s a testament to how much you loved Marcus—how deeply he was part of your world. His jokes, his teasing about your cooking, the way he’d show up with his silly grin—those were the threads of love, of connection, and they still matter. They’re not gone. They’re part of who you are now, and they’ll live on in your memories, in the way you laugh at his old jokes, in the way you cook his favorite meal even though it’s not the same without him.

Your parents are hurting too, and you’re already doing something profoundly brave by holding things together for them. But please remember: you don’t have to carry it all alone. It’s okay to ask for help—whether it’s a friend, a therapist, a grief support group, or even just someone to sit with you in silence while you feel the weight. You don’t have to be strong all the time. You can be broken, and still be loved.

Marcus is gone, but his presence isn’t. He’s in the stories you tell, in the quiet moments when you catch a scent or hear a song that reminds you of him.
